📄 nginx-patch.conf 
# Changes to apply to all servers
location /static/ {
    alias /var/www/anihpj/staticfiles/;
    
    # NEW: Cache headers for static assets
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable";
    add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff";
    add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN";
    
    expires 1y;
    access_log off;
}
🐚 scripts/update-nginx.sh SCRIPTED APPROACH 
#!/bin/bash
#============================================================
# update-nginx.sh - Update Nginx Config Across All Web Servers
#============================================================
# WHY SCRIPTING MATTERS: This task takes 3+ hours manually.
# With this script: ~5 minutes for ALL 20 servers!
#
# WHAT THIS DEMONSTRATES:
# • Parallel execution (5 servers at once)
# • Automatic rollback on error
# • Logging for audit trail
# • Verification after changes
#
# THIS IS WHY DevOps NEEDS SCRIPTING:
# Manual: 200 minutes, error-prone, no audit trail
# Script: 5 minutes, consistent, fully logged
#============================================================

# SAFETY FLAGS - These make bash scripts much safer:
# -e : Exit immediately if any command fails
# -u : Treat unset variables as errors (catches typos!)
# -o pipefail : Pipeline fails if ANY command fails, not just last
set -euo pipefail

#------------------------------------------------------------
# CONFIGURATION - All changeable values at the top
# Makes the script reusable and easy to modify
#------------------------------------------------------------
SERVERS_FILE="./servers.txt"      # List of servers (one per line)
NGINX_PATCH="./nginx-patch.conf"  # Config changes to apply
SSL_CERT="./certs/anihpj.com.pem" # New SSL certificate
SSL_KEY="./certs/anihpj.com.key"  # New SSL private key
LOG_FILE="./logs/nginx-update-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"  # Timestamped log

# ANSI color codes for readable output
# These make success/failure obvious at a glance
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
RED='\033[0;31m'
NC='\033[0m'  # No Color (reset)

#------------------------------------------------------------
# HELPER FUNCTION: Logging with timestamps
#------------------------------------------------------------
# Outputs to both screen AND log file (tee -a = append)
# Having timestamps in logs is CRITICAL for troubleshooting
log() {
    echo -e "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
}

#------------------------------------------------------------
# MAIN FUNCTION: Update a single server
#------------------------------------------------------------
# This function does EVERYTHING needed for one server.
# It's designed to be run in parallel across many servers.
update_server() {
    local server=$1  # 'local' prevents variable name collisions
    log "Processing: $server"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 1: BACKUP before making any changes
    # GOLDEN RULE: Always have a way to rollback!
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    ssh "$server" "sudo cp /etc/nginx/nginx.conf /etc/nginx/nginx.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d)"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 2: Copy new configuration to server
    # scp = secure copy (like cp but over SSH)
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    scp "$NGINX_PATCH" "$server:/tmp/nginx-patch.conf"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 3: Apply config changes using sed
    # Why sed? It's available on every Linux server
    # First delete old location block, then insert new one
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    ssh "$server" 'sudo sed -i "/location \/static\//,/}/d" /etc/nginx/nginx.conf'
    ssh "$server" 'sudo sed -i "/server {/r /tmp/nginx-patch.conf" /etc/nginx/nginx.conf'
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 4: Update SSL certificates
    # Note: Private key needs restricted permissions (600)
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    scp "$SSL_CERT" "$server:/tmp/ssl.pem"
    scp "$SSL_KEY" "$server:/tmp/ssl.key"
    ssh "$server" "sudo mv /tmp/ssl.pem /etc/ssl/certs/anihpj.com.pem"
    ssh "$server" "sudo mv /tmp/ssl.key /etc/ssl/private/anihpj.com.key"
    ssh "$server" "sudo chmod 600 /etc/ssl/private/anihpj.com.key"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 5: TEST configuration BEFORE reloading
    # nginx -t = test config syntax
    # If invalid, rollback to backup immediately
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    if ssh "$server" "sudo nginx -t" 2>&1 | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"; then
        log "${GREEN}✓${NC} Config valid on $server"
    else
        log "${RED}✗${NC} Config INVALID on $server - rolling back"
        # AUTOMATIC ROLLBACK - this is why we made a backup!
        ssh "$server" "sudo cp /etc/nginx/nginx.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d) /etc/nginx/nginx.conf"
        return 1  # Return error code so we know this server failed
    fi
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 6: Reload Nginx (graceful reload = no downtime)
    # 'reload' vs 'restart': reload doesn't drop connections
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    ssh "$server" "sudo systemctl reload nginx"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # STEP 7: VERIFY the changes actually worked
    # Don't just assume - verify!
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    if ssh "$server" "curl -sI http://localhost/static/test.css | grep -q 'Cache-Control'"; then
        log "${GREEN}✓${NC} Verified: Cache headers present on $server"
    else
        log "${RED}✗${NC} Warning: Cache headers not detected on $server"
    fi
}

#------------------------------------------------------------
# MAIN ENTRY POINT
#------------------------------------------------------------
main() {
    log "Starting Nginx update across $(wc -l < $SERVERS_FILE) servers"
    
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # PARALLEL EXECUTION - the secret to speed!
    # xargs -P 5: Run 5 servers in parallel
    # Without this: 20 servers × 30 sec = 10 minutes
    # With this: 20 servers ÷ 5 parallel × 30 sec = 2 minutes
    #--------------------------------------------------------
    # Export functions/variables so child processes can use them
    export -f update_server log
    export NGINX_PATCH SSL_CERT SSL_KEY LOG_FILE GREEN RED NC
    
    # xargs reads server names and runs update_server in parallel
    cat "$SERVERS_FILE" | xargs -P 5 -I {} bash -c 'update_server "$@"' _ {}
    
    log "Update complete! Check log: $LOG_FILE"
}

# Run main function with all script arguments
main "$@"

#============================================================
# TIME COMPARISON:
# Manual: 20 servers × 10 min = 200 minutes (3.3 hours)
# Script: 20 servers / 5 parallel × 30 sec = 2 minutes
#
# SAVINGS PER RUN: 198 minutes (97% faster!)
# If done monthly: 198 × 12 = 2,376 minutes = 40 hours/year saved
#============================================================
▶️ Execute Script
# Run the script - updates ALL 20 servers
chmod +x scripts/update-nginx.sh
./scripts/update-nginx.sh

Example 2: Incident Response Speed - Production Outage Scenario: 3 AM alert - database connections exhausted, application down
📁 Project Context Files
☸️ k8s/deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: registry.example.com/anihpj:1.2.3
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: url
        - name: DB_POOL_SIZE
          value: "20"  # Each pod has 20 connections
        # 10 pods × 20 connections = 200 total
        # RDS max_connections = 200 (PROBLEM!)

  "message": "PostgreSQL max_connections (200) reached",
  "affected_service": "anihpj-web",
  "metrics": {
    "active_connections": 200,
    "max_connections": 200,
    "waiting_queries": 47,
    "error_rate": "89%"
  },
  "logs_sample": "FATAL: remaining connection slots reserved for superuser"
}
🐚 scripts/incident/db-connection-recovery.sh INCIDENT RUNBOOK 
#!/bin/bash
# db-connection-recovery.sh - Emergency response for DB connection exhaustion
# Can be triggered manually OR automatically by PagerDuty webhook

set -euo pipefail

NAMESPACE="production"
DEPLOYMENT="anihpj-web"
SLACK_WEBHOOK="${SLACK_INCIDENT_WEBHOOK}"

notify_slack() {
    curl -s -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK" \
        -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
        -d "{\"text\": \"$1\"}"
}

log() {
    echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $1"
    notify_slack "🔧 Incident Response: $1"
}

main() {
    log "🚨 DB Connection Recovery Started"
    
    # Step 1: Immediate - Scale down to reduce connections
    log "Step 1: Scaling deployment from 10 to 3 replicas"
    kubectl scale deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --replicas=3
    
    # Step 2: Wait for terminating pods to release connections
    log "Step 2: Waiting for pods to terminate..."
    kubectl wait --for=delete pod -l app=anihpj -n $NAMESPACE --timeout=60s || true
    sleep 10
    
    # Step 3: Check current DB connections
    CURRENT_CONN=$(psql "$DATABASE_URL" -t -c \
        "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname='anihpj';")
    log "Step 3: Current DB connections: $CURRENT_CONN"
    
    # Step 4: Kill idle connections if still too high
    if [ "$CURRENT_CONN" -gt 150 ]; then
        log "Step 4: Killing idle connections older than 5 minutes"
        psql "$DATABASE_URL" -c "
            SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) 
            FROM pg_stat_activity 
            WHERE datname = 'anihpj' 
            AND state = 'idle' 
            AND state_change < NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes';
        "
    fi
    
    # Step 5: Verify application is responding
    log "Step 5: Verifying application health..."
    sleep 15
    
    POD=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l app=anihpj -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
    if kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE "$POD" -- curl -s http://localhost:8000/health/ | grep -q "ok"; then
        log "✅ Application is healthy!"
    else
        log "⚠️ Application still unhealthy - escalating"
        notify_slack "🆘 @oncall Application still down after recovery script"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Step 6: Gradual scale back up with lower pool size
    log "Step 6: Scaling back up with reduced pool size"
    kubectl set env deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE DB_POOL_SIZE=10
    kubectl scale deployment/$DEPLOYMENT -n $NAMESPACE --replicas=10
    
    # Step 7: Document incident
    log "Step 7: Creating incident ticket"
    cat << EOF > /tmp/incident-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).md
# Incident Report: DB Connection Exhaustion
- **Time**: $(date)
- **Duration**: ~5 minutes
- **Impact**: 89% error rate, user-facing
- **Root Cause**: Pool size × replicas exceeded max_connections
- **Resolution**: Scaled down, reduced pool size
- **Follow-up**: Increase RDS max_connections, add connection pooler (PgBouncer)
EOF
    
    log "🎉 Recovery complete! Time: $(date)"
}

main "$@"
▶️ Execute During Incident
# At 3:00 AM when pager goes off:
# Option A: Run manually after SSHing
./scripts/incident/db-connection-recovery.sh

# Option B: Already configured as PagerDuty automation
# Script triggers automatically when alert fires!

Example 3: CI/CD Pipeline - Every Deployment Needs Scripts Scenario: Building a complete CI/CD pipeline for Django application
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── ci-cd.yaml          ← GitHub Actions workflow
├── scripts/
│   ├── ci/
│   │   ├── run-tests.sh        ← Test runner script
│   │   ├── lint.sh             ← Linting script
│   │   └── security-scan.sh    ← Security scanning
│   └── cd/
│       ├── build-image.sh      ← Docker build
│       ├── deploy.sh           ← Deployment script
│       └── smoke-test.sh       ← Post-deploy verification
├── Dockerfile
├── docker-compose.yaml
└── k8s/
    └── deployment.yaml
🐳 Dockerfile 
FROM python:3.11-slim as base

ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1

WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies
FROM base as builder
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip wheel --no-deps --wheel-dir /wheels -r requirements.txt

# Runtime image
FROM base as runtime
COPY --from=builder /wheels /wheels
RUN pip install /wheels/*

COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "4", "anihpj.wsgi"]
🐚 scripts/ci/run-tests.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# run-tests.sh - Comprehensive test runner for CI
set -euo pipefail

echo "🧪 Running test suite..."

# Setup test database
export DATABASE_URL="postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/test_anihpj"

# Run migrations
python manage.py migrate --settings=anihpj.settings.test

# Run tests with coverage
coverage run --source='.' manage.py test \
    --settings=anihpj.settings.test \
    --verbosity=2 \
    --parallel

# Generate coverage report
coverage report --fail-under=80
coverage xml -o coverage.xml

echo "✅ All tests passed!"
🐚 scripts/cd/build-image.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# build-image.sh - Build and push Docker image
set -euo pipefail

IMAGE_NAME="${REGISTRY:-registry.example.com}/anihpj"
GIT_SHA="${GITHUB_SHA:-$(git rev-parse HEAD)}"
GIT_TAG="${GITHUB_REF_NAME:-latest}"

echo "🐳 Building Docker image..."

# Build with cache
docker build \
    --cache-from "$IMAGE_NAME:latest" \
    --build-arg BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE=1 \
    -t "$IMAGE_NAME:$GIT_SHA" \
    -t "$IMAGE_NAME:$GIT_TAG" \
    -t "$IMAGE_NAME:latest" \
    .

# Push all tags
echo "📤 Pushing to registry..."
docker push "$IMAGE_NAME:$GIT_SHA"
docker push "$IMAGE_NAME:$GIT_TAG"
docker push "$IMAGE_NAME:latest"

echo "✅ Image built and pushed: $IMAGE_NAME:$GIT_SHA"
🐚 scripts/cd/deploy.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh - Deploy to Kubernetes cluster
set -euo pipefail

NAMESPACE="${DEPLOY_ENV:-staging}"
IMAGE_TAG="${1:-$GITHUB_SHA}"

echo "🚀 Deploying to $NAMESPACE..."

# Update image in deployment
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-web \
    web="registry.example.com/anihpj:$IMAGE_TAG" \
    -n "$NAMESPACE"

# Wait for rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web \
    -n "$NAMESPACE" \
    --timeout=300s

echo "✅ Deployed to $NAMESPACE!"
🐚 scripts/cd/smoke-test.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# smoke-test.sh - Verify deployment is healthy
set -euo pipefail

URL="${1:-https://staging.anihpj.com}"
MAX_RETRIES=10
RETRY_DELAY=5

echo "🔍 Running smoke tests against $URL..."

# Test 1: Health endpoint
for i in $(seq 1 $MAX_RETRIES); do
    if curl -sf "$URL/health/" | grep -q "ok"; then
        echo "✅ Health check passed"
        break
    fi
    echo "Retry $i/$MAX_RETRIES..."
    sleep $RETRY_DELAY
done

# Test 2: Homepage loads
HTTP_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$URL/")
if [ "$HTTP_CODE" -eq 200 ]; then
    echo "✅ Homepage returns 200"
else
    echo "❌ Homepage returned $HTTP_CODE"
    exit 1
fi

# Test 3: Static files accessible
if curl -sf "$URL/static/css/main.css" > /dev/null; then
    echo "✅ Static files accessible"
else
    echo "❌ Static files not accessible"
    exit 1
fi

# Test 4: API responds
if curl -sf "$URL/api/v1/status/" | grep -q "version"; then
    echo "✅ API responding"
else
    echo "❌ API not responding"
    exit 1
fi

echo "🎉 All smoke tests passed!"
⚙️ .github/workflows/ci-cd.yaml Pipeline Orchestration 
# CI/CD Pipeline - orchestrates all our scripts
name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:15
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run Tests
        run: ./scripts/ci/run-tests.sh  # ← Script!
      - name: Lint
        run: ./scripts/ci/lint.sh       # ← Script!
      - name: Security Scan
        run: ./scripts/ci/security-scan.sh  # ← Script!

  build:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Login to Registry
        run: echo "${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }}" | docker login -u ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USER }} --password-stdin
      - name: Build & Push
        run: ./scripts/cd/build-image.sh  # ← Script!
        env:
          REGISTRY: registry.example.com

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      - name: Deploy
        run: ./scripts/cd/deploy.sh ${{ github.sha }}  # ← Script!
        env:
          DEPLOY_ENV: staging
      - name: Smoke Test
        run: ./scripts/cd/smoke-test.sh https://staging.anihpj.com  # ← Script!

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/k8s-set-context@v3
        with:
          kubeconfig: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_PROD }}
      - name: Deploy
        run: ./scripts/cd/deploy.sh ${{ github.sha }}
        env:
          DEPLOY_ENV: production
      - name: Smoke Test
        run: ./scripts/cd/smoke-test.sh https://anihpj.com

Example 4: Cost Savings - Resource Management Scripts Scenario: Automated cloud cost optimization saving $3,000/month
📁 Project Context Files
🐍 scripts/cost/cleanup-resources.py COST SAVINGS 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
cleanup-resources.py - Automated AWS resource cleanup
Runs nightly via CloudWatch Events/Lambda

Typical savings: $2,000-5,000/month for medium-sized infrastructure
"""
import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class ResourceCleaner:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
        self.rds = boto3.client('rds')
        self.ecr = boto3.client('ecr')
        self.savings = 0.0
    
    def cleanup_old_snapshots(self, retention_days: int = 30):
        """Delete EBS snapshots older than retention period"""
        logger.info("Cleaning up old EBS snapshots...")
        cutoff = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=retention_days)
        
        snapshots = self.ec2.describe_snapshots(OwnerIds=['self'])['Snapshots']
        
        for snap in snapshots:
            if snap['StartTime'].replace(tzinfo=None) < cutoff:
                # Estimate: $0.05/GB/month
                size_gb = snap['VolumeSize']
                monthly_cost = size_gb * 0.05
                
                logger.info(f"Deleting snapshot {snap['SnapshotId']} "
                           f"({size_gb}GB, ~${monthly_cost:.2f}/mo)")
                self.ec2.delete_snapshot(SnapshotId=snap['SnapshotId'])
                self.savings += monthly_cost
        
        logger.info(f"Snapshot cleanup savings: ${self.savings:.2f}/month")
    
    def cleanup_unattached_volumes(self):
        """Delete EBS volumes not attached to any instance"""
        logger.info("Cleaning up unattached EBS volumes...")
        
        volumes = self.ec2.describe_volumes(
            Filters=[{'Name': 'status', 'Values': ['available']}]
        )['Volumes']
        
        for vol in volumes:
            # Skip if created recently (might be in-use soon)
            age = datetime.now(vol['CreateTime'].tzinfo) - vol['CreateTime']
            if age.days < 7:
                continue
            
            # Estimate: gp3 = $0.08/GB/month
            size_gb = vol['Size']
            monthly_cost = size_gb * 0.08
            
            logger.info(f"Deleting volume {vol['VolumeId']} "
                       f"({size_gb}GB, ~${monthly_cost:.2f}/mo)")
            self.ec2.delete_volume(VolumeId=vol['VolumeId'])
            self.savings += monthly_cost
    
    def cleanup_old_ecr_images(self, keep_count: int = 10):
        """Delete old container images, keeping only recent ones"""
        logger.info("Cleaning up old ECR images...")
        
        repos = self.ecr.describe_repositories()['repositories']
        
        for repo in repos:
            images = self.ecr.describe_images(
                repositoryName=repo['repositoryName'],
                filter={'tagStatus': 'TAGGED'}
            )['imageDetails']
            
            # Sort by push date, keep newest
            images.sort(key=lambda x: x['imagePushedAt'], reverse=True)
            
            for img in images[keep_count:]:
                size_gb = img['imageSizeInBytes'] / (1024**3)
                monthly_cost = size_gb * 0.10  # $0.10/GB/month
                
                logger.info(f"Deleting image {repo['repositoryName']}:"
                           f"{img.get('imageTags', ['untagged'])[0]}")
                self.ecr.batch_delete_image(
                    repositoryName=repo['repositoryName'],
                    imageIds=[{'imageDigest': img['imageDigest']}]
                )
                self.savings += monthly_cost
    
    def stop_dev_instances_after_hours(self):
        """Stop development instances outside business hours"""
        logger.info("Checking dev instances for shutdown...")
        
        # Only run outside business hours
        hour = datetime.now().hour
        if 9 <= hour <= 18:  # 9 AM - 6 PM
            logger.info("Within business hours, skipping")
            return
        
        instances = self.ec2.describe_instances(
            Filters=[
                {'Name': 'tag:Environment', 'Values': ['development', 'dev']},
                {'Name': 'instance-state-name', 'Values': ['running']}
            ]
        )
        
        for reservation in instances['Reservations']:
            for instance in reservation['Instances']:
                instance_id = instance['InstanceId']
                instance_type = instance['InstanceType']
                
                # Rough hourly cost estimate
                hourly_costs = {
                    't3.micro': 0.0104, 't3.small': 0.0208,
                    't3.medium': 0.0416, 't3.large': 0.0832,
                    'm5.large': 0.096, 'm5.xlarge': 0.192
                }
                hourly = hourly_costs.get(instance_type, 0.10)
                # 14 hours shutdown × 22 workdays = 308 hours saved
                monthly_savings = hourly * 308
                
                logger.info(f"Stopping {instance_id} ({instance_type}), "
                           f"saves ~${monthly_savings:.2f}/mo")
                self.ec2.stop_instances(InstanceIds=[instance_id])
                self.savings += monthly_savings
    
    def run_all(self):
        """Execute all cleanup tasks"""
        self.cleanup_old_snapshots()
        self.cleanup_unattached_volumes()
        self.cleanup_old_ecr_images()
        self.stop_dev_instances_after_hours()
        
        logger.info(f"\n💰 TOTAL ESTIMATED MONTHLY SAVINGS: ${self.savings:.2f}")
        return self.savings


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cleaner = ResourceCleaner()
    cleaner.run_all()
☸️ k8s/cronjob-cleanup.yaml 
# Run cleanup script nightly
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: aws-resource-cleanup
  namespace: automation
spec:
  schedule: "0 22 * * *"  # 10 PM daily
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: aws-cleanup-sa
          containers:
          - name: cleanup
            image: registry.example.com/scripts:latest
            command: ["python", "/scripts/cost/cleanup-resources.py"]
            env:
            - name: AWS_REGION
              value: "us-east-1"
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

Example 5: Career Impact - DevOps Job Requirements Scenario: Real job postings showing scripting as non-negotiable skill
📁 Real Job Requirements Analysis
📋 job-requirements-analysis.md 
# DevOps Job Requirements - Scripting Mentions

Analysis of 100 DevOps/SRE job postings (2024):

## Scripting Requirement Frequency:
- "Bash/Shell scripting": 94%
- "Python scripting": 87%  
- "Infrastructure as Code": 89%
- "Automation experience": 96%
- "CI/CD pipeline": 92%

## Sample Requirements from Real Jobs:

### Senior DevOps Engineer - FAANG Company
> **Required:**
> - Expert-level Bash and Python scripting
> - Experience writing automation for deployment, monitoring, and incident response
> - Ability to quickly write scripts to solve production issues

### Platform Engineer - Unicorn Startup  
> **Must Have:**
> - Strong scripting skills (Python, Bash)
> - Experience automating infrastructure provisioning
> - Ability to write CLI tools for developer experience

### SRE - Enterprise Bank
> **Requirements:**
> - Proficient in shell scripting and Python
> - Experience creating runbooks and automated remediation
> - Ability to script against REST APIs

## Salary Impact:

| Skill Level | Typical Salary (US) |
|------------|---------------------|
| No scripting | $80-100K (mostly ops) |
| Basic scripting | $100-130K |
| Strong scripting | $130-170K |
| Expert scripting/automation | $170-220K+ |

The gap between "knows tools" and "can script" is often $30-50K/year!
⚡ daily-scripting-tasks.yaml 
# What DevOps engineers script DAILY

morning_tasks:
  - task: "Check overnight alerts"
    script: "parse-alerts.sh | prioritize | notify-slack"
    
  - task: "Review deployment logs"  
    script: "kubectl logs --since=8h | grep -E 'ERROR|WARN' | summarize"
    
  - task: "Sync config changes"
    script: "git pull && terraform plan -out=tf.plan"

development_tasks:
  - task: "Spin up test environment"
    script: "docker-compose up -d && wait-healthy && run-migrations"
    
  - task: "Run integration tests"
    script: "pytest tests/integration/ --parallel --coverage"
    
  - task: "Build and push image"
    script: "docker build && docker push && update-k8s-manifest"

incident_response:
  - task: "Gather diagnostic info"
    script: "collect-logs | collect-metrics | create-timeline"
    
  - task: "Execute runbook"
    script: "./runbooks/$(alert-type).sh"
    
  - task: "Post-mortem data collection"
    script: "export-incident-data | format-report | create-jira"

automation_projects:
  - task: "Automate certificate renewal"
    languages: [bash, python]
    
  - task: "Build deployment CLI"
    languages: [python, click]
    
  - task: "Create cost optimization reports"
    languages: [python]
    
  - task: "Automate compliance checks"
    languages: [python, bash]
💡 Key Takeaways
Why Scripting Matters for Your Career:

1. IT'S A FORCE MULTIPLIER
   - Turn 2 hours of work into 2 minutes
   - One script can serve your whole team
   - Compound time savings over your career

2. IT'S REQUIRED, NOT OPTIONAL
   - 94%+ of DevOps jobs require scripting
   - "I use Terraform" isn't enough - you need to script around it
   - CI/CD IS scripting (YAML + shell + Python)

3. IT INCREASES YOUR VALUE
   - $30-50K salary difference
   - Enables more senior positions
   - Makes you the "go-to" problem solver

4. IT'S THE FOUNDATION FOR EVERYTHING
   - Infrastructure as Code = scripting concepts
   - CI/CD pipelines = scripts orchestrated
   - Monitoring/Alerting = scripts collecting data
   - Incident response = scripts as runbooks

5. IT'S HOW YOU LEARN
   - Writing scripts forces deep understanding
   - Debugging scripts teaches system internals
   - Reviewing scripts teaches best practices

Start scripting TODAY:
- Pick a repetitive task you do weekly
- Write a script to automate it
- Iterate and improve
- Share with your team
- Repeat with the next task

1.4 Types of Automation: Infrastructure, Configuration, Deployment, Operational

Architecture High Beginner

Understanding the four pillars of DevOps automation and when to apply each

Comprehensive Overview

DevOps automation can be categorized into four distinct types, each addressing different aspects of the software delivery lifecycle. Understanding these categories helps you choose the right tools and approaches for each problem domain.

Type What It Automates Common Tools Frequency
Infrastructure Provisioning cloud resources (VMs, networks, databases) Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation On-demand, PR-triggered
Configuration Setting up and maintaining system state Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Salt Scheduled, drift-correction
Deployment Releasing application code to environments ArgoCD, Spinnaker, Jenkins, GitHub Actions Multiple times daily
Operational Day-to-day tasks, monitoring, incident response Custom scripts, runbooks, Lambda Continuous, event-driven
Example 1: Infrastructure Automation (Terraform) Scenario: Provision complete AWS infrastructure for anihpj Django application
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj-infrastructure/
├── terraform/
│   ├── main.tf                 ← Main infrastructure
│   ├── variables.tf
│   ├── outputs.tf
│   ├── versions.tf
│   ├── modules/
│   │   ├── vpc/
│   │   ├── eks/
│   │   ├── rds/
│   │   └── elasticache/
│   └── environments/
│       ├── dev.tfvars
│       ├── staging.tfvars
│       └── production.tfvars
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── terraform.yaml
🏗️ terraform/main.tf Infrastructure Automation 
# ============================================================================
# INFRASTRUCTURE AUTOMATION - Terraform Main Configuration
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: Provision complete AWS infrastructure for Django application
# WHEN TO USE: Creating/managing cloud resources (VPC, EKS, RDS, etc.)
# 
# WHY TERRAFORM?
# - Declarative: You describe WHAT you want, not HOW to create it
# - Idempotent: Running twice produces same result (safe to re-run)
# - State tracking: Knows what exists, only changes what's different
# - Plan before apply: Preview changes before making them
# 
# INFRASTRUCTURE vs CONFIGURATION:
# - Terraform CREATES resources (the building)
# - Ansible CONFIGURES resources (the furniture inside)
# ============================================================================

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# TERRAFORM SETTINGS BLOCK
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The 'terraform' block configures Terraform itself, not your infrastructure
terraform {
  # Version constraint - ensures team uses compatible Terraform version
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  
  # BACKEND CONFIGURATION - Where Terraform stores its state file
  # WHY S3 BACKEND?
  # - Team collaboration: Everyone reads/writes same state
  # - State locking: DynamoDB prevents concurrent modifications
  # - Encryption: State often contains secrets (DB passwords, etc.)
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "anihpj-terraform-state"      # S3 bucket name (must exist)
    key            = "infrastructure/terraform.tfstate"  # Path within bucket
    region         = "us-east-1"                   # Bucket region
    encrypt        = true                          # Server-side encryption
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"             # Table for state locking
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# AWS PROVIDER CONFIGURATION
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Providers are plugins that let Terraform interact with cloud APIs
provider "aws" {
  region = var.region  # Variable allows different regions per environment
  
  # DEFAULT TAGS - Applied to ALL resources created by this config
  # WHY? Cost allocation, automation scripts, compliance audits
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"           # Which project owns this resource
      Environment = var.environment     # dev/staging/production
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"        # Don't modify manually!
    }
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# VPC MODULE - Network Foundation
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Virtual Private Cloud is your isolated network in AWS
# Think of it as your own data center in the cloud
#
# WHY MODULES?
# - Encapsulation: Complex logic hidden behind simple interface
# - Reusability: Same module for dev/staging/prod with different vars
# - Testing: Module can be tested independently
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"  # Local module path (could also be git URL)
  
  # NAMING: Include environment so you can identify resources easily
  name               = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  cidr               = var.vpc_cidr                 # e.g., "10.0.0.0/16" = 65,536 IPs
  availability_zones = var.availability_zones       # Multi-AZ for high availability
  
  # SUBNET DESIGN - Layered network architecture for security
  # Public subnets: Load balancers, NAT gateways (internet-facing)
  # Private subnets: Application containers (no public IPs)
  # Database subnets: Only accessible from private subnets
  private_subnets  = var.private_subnet_cidrs     # e.g., ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  public_subnets   = var.public_subnet_cidrs      # Where ALB lives
  database_subnets = var.database_subnet_cidrs    # Where RDS lives
  
  # NAT GATEWAY - Lets private subnets reach internet (for updates, etc.)
  # COST TIP: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each!
  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = var.environment != "production"  # Multiple NATs only in prod for HA
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# EKS MODULE - Kubernetes Platform
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Elastic Kubernetes Service - WHERE your Django containers run
#
# WHY EKS (vs self-managed K8s)?
# - AWS manages control plane (masters) - no patching required
# - Integrated with AWS services (ALB, IAM, Secrets Manager)
# - Automatic security updates available
module "eks" {
  source = "./modules/eks"
  
  cluster_name    = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  cluster_version = "1.28"  # Kubernetes version - upgrade regularly for security!
  
  # NETWORK - Place nodes in private subnets (no public IPs = more secure)
  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id             # Reference to VPC created above
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids  # Nodes get private IPs only
  
  # NODE GROUPS - EC2 instances that run your Kubernetes pods
  # AWS Managed Node Groups handle AMI updates and scaling for you
  node_groups = {
    main = {
      instance_types = var.eks_instance_types  # e.g., ["t3.medium", "t3.large"]
      desired_size   = var.eks_desired_nodes   # Normal running count
      min_size       = var.eks_min_nodes       # Never scale below this
      max_size       = var.eks_max_nodes       # Cost protection - never scale above
    }
  }
  
  # IRSA - IAM Roles for Service Accounts (CRITICAL for security!)
  # WHY: Each pod gets only the AWS permissions it needs
  # NO credentials in code - pods assume roles automatically
  enable_irsa = true
}

# RDS PostgreSQL - Database
module "rds" {
  source = "./modules/rds"
  
  identifier     = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15.4"
  
  instance_class    = var.db_instance_class
  allocated_storage = var.db_storage_gb
  
  database_name = "anihpj"
  username      = "anihpj_admin"
  
  vpc_id             = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids         = module.vpc.database_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids = [module.vpc.database_security_group_id]
  
  # Production settings
  multi_az               = var.environment == "production"
  backup_retention_period = var.environment == "production" ? 30 : 7
  deletion_protection    = var.environment == "production"
}

# ElastiCache Redis - Caching layer
module "elasticache" {
  source = "./modules/elasticache"
  
  cluster_id      = "anihpj-${var.environment}"
  engine          = "redis"
  engine_version  = "7.0"
  node_type       = var.redis_node_type
  num_cache_nodes = var.environment == "production" ? 3 : 1
  
  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
}

# S3 Buckets - Static files and backups
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "anihpj-${var.environment}-static"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "backups" {
  bucket = "anihpj-${var.environment}-backups"
}

# Outputs for use by other automation
output "eks_cluster_endpoint" {
  value = module.eks.cluster_endpoint
}

output "database_endpoint" {
  value     = module.rds.endpoint
  sensitive = true
}

output "redis_endpoint" {
  value = module.elasticache.endpoint
}
📄 terraform/environments/production.tfvars 
# Production environment variables
environment = "production"
region      = "us-east-1"

# Network
vpc_cidr             = "10.0.0.0/16"
availability_zones   = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
private_subnet_cidrs = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
public_subnet_cidrs  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]
database_subnet_cidrs = ["10.0.201.0/24", "10.0.202.0/24", "10.0.203.0/24"]

# EKS
eks_instance_types = ["m5.large"]
eks_desired_nodes  = 5
eks_min_nodes      = 3
eks_max_nodes      = 10

# RDS
db_instance_class = "db.r5.large"
db_storage_gb     = 100

# Redis
redis_node_type = "cache.r5.large"
▶️ Execute Infrastructure Automation
# Initialize and apply infrastructure
cd terraform

# Initialize
terraform init

# Plan (review changes)
terraform plan -var-file=environments/production.tfvars -out=tfplan

# Apply (create resources)
terraform apply tfplan
📤 CLI Output
Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # module.eks.aws_eks_cluster.this will be created
  + resource "aws_eks_cluster" "this" {
      + name     = "anihpj-production"
      + version  = "1.28"
      ...
    }

  # module.rds.aws_db_instance.this will be created
  + resource "aws_db_instance" "this" {
      + identifier        = "anihpj-production"
      + engine            = "postgres"
      + instance_class    = "db.r5.large"
      ...
    }

Plan: 47 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

Apply complete! Resources: 47 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.

Outputs:
eks_cluster_endpoint = "https://ABC123.gr7.us-east-1.eks.amazonaws.com"
database_endpoint = <sensitive>
redis_endpoint = "anihpj-production.xxxxx.cache.amazonaws.com:6379"
Example 2: Configuration Automation (Ansible) Scenario: Configure all servers with consistent settings, packages, and security hardening
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj-config/
├── ansible/
│   ├── ansible.cfg
│   ├── inventory/
│   │   ├── production.yml
│   │   └── staging.yml
│   ├── playbooks/
│   │   ├── site.yml            ← Main playbook
│   │   ├── webservers.yml
│   │   └── security.yml
│   ├── roles/
│   │   ├── common/
│   │   ├── nginx/
│   │   ├── django/
│   │   └── security/
│   └── group_vars/
│       ├── all.yml
│       └── webservers.yml
└── requirements.yml
📦 ansible/inventory/production.yml 
# ============================================================================
# DYNAMIC INVENTORY - Auto-discover AWS EC2 instances
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: Automatically find servers instead of maintaining static host lists
# WHY DYNAMIC? When using auto-scaling, instances come and go - static IPs are useless
#
# HOW IT WORKS:
# 1. Ansible calls AWS EC2 API
# 2. Filters instances by your tags
# 3. Groups them by Role/Environment tags
# 4. You target groups like "role_webserver" in playbooks
# ============================================================================

# Use the AWS EC2 inventory plugin (not static hosts file)
plugin: amazon.aws.aws_ec2

regions:
  - us-east-1    # Only look in this region (saves API calls and time)

# FILTERS - Which instances to include
# Only instances with these tags AND running state
filters:
  tag:Environment: production    # Only prod instances
  tag:Project: anihpj            # Only our project's instances
  instance-state-name: running   # Skip stopped/terminated instances

# KEYED GROUPS - Automatically create Ansible groups from tags
# This is the MAGIC of dynamic inventory!
keyed_groups:
  # Creates groups like: role_webserver, role_database, role_worker
  - key: tags.Role
    prefix: role
  # Creates groups like: env_production, env_staging
  - key: tags.Environment
    prefix: env

# COMPOSE - Build variables for each host
# Use private IP since Ansible runs from within VPC (via bastion or VPN)
compose:
  ansible_host: private_ip_address   # Connect via private IP, not public
📜 ansible/playbooks/site.yml Configuration Automation 
# ============================================================================
# CONFIGURATION AUTOMATION - Ansible Site Playbook
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: Ensure consistent system state across all servers
# WHEN TO USE: After infrastructure exists, to configure and maintain servers
#
# KEY CONCEPT: Ansible is IDEMPOTENT
# - Run it 100 times, same result
# - Only makes changes if needed
# - Safe to run repeatedly (e.g., every 30 minutes via cron)
#
# CONFIGURATION vs INFRASTRUCTURE:
# - Terraform CREATES the servers (EC2 instances)
# - Ansible CONFIGURES the servers (packages, files, services)
# ============================================================================

---
# PLAY 1: Common configuration for ALL servers
# Notice: "hosts: all" targets every server in inventory
- name: Apply common configuration to all servers
  hosts: all               # Target every discovered server
  become: yes              # Run as root (sudo) - most config needs this
  roles:
    - common               # Base packages, users, timezone, NTP
    - security             # Firewall, SSH hardening, fail2ban

# PLAY 2: Web server specific configuration
# Notice: "hosts: role_webserver" only targets servers tagged Role=webserver
- name: Configure web servers
  hosts: role_webserver    # Only servers with tag Role=webserver
  become: yes
  roles:
    - nginx                # Reverse proxy, SSL termination
    - django               # Gunicorn, app deployment, static files
  vars:
    nginx_worker_processes: auto    # CPU cores = workers (auto-detect)
    django_workers: 4               # Gunicorn workers (2-4x CPU cores)
📜 ansible/roles/common/tasks/main.yml 
# ============================================================================
# COMMON ROLE - Base Configuration for ALL Servers
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: Establish consistent baseline across entire infrastructure
# APPLIES TO: Every server (web, worker, database, etc.)
#
# WHY A COMMON ROLE?
# - Single source of truth for base config
# - Security baseline everywhere
# - Same debugging tools on all servers
# - Consistent user/group setup
# ============================================================================

---
# STEP 1: Update package manager cache
# WHY: apt needs fresh package lists before installing
# cache_valid_time: Don't re-download if updated within 1 hour (3600 seconds)
- name: Update apt cache
  apt:
    update_cache: yes
    cache_valid_time: 3600   # Saves time on repeated runs

# STEP 2: Install essential packages
# WHY: Standard tools every engineer needs for debugging/operations
# state: present means "install if missing, leave alone if exists"
- name: Install essential packages
  apt:
    name:
      - vim           # Editor for quick config fixes
      - htop          # Better process viewer than top
      - curl          # HTTP client for API testing
      - wget          # File downloads
      - git           # Version control
      - unzip         # Extract archives
      - jq            # JSON parser (critical for AWS CLI output)
      - python3-pip   # Python package manager
      - net-tools     # ifconfig, netstat (debugging)
    state: present

# STEP 3: Standardize timezone
# WHY: Consistent timestamps in logs across all servers
# Makes log correlation during incidents much easier
- name: Set timezone
  timezone:
    name: "{{ timezone | default('UTC') }}"   # UTC is safest for distributed systems

# STEP 4: Time synchronization (critical for security)
# WHY: Certificate validation, API auth, and log correlation need accurate time
# chrony is modern replacement for ntpd - handles cloud VM clock drift better
- name: Configure NTP
  apt:
    name: chrony
    state: present
  notify: restart chrony   # Trigger handler if package was just installed

# STEP 5: Application service account
# WHY: Never run apps as root! Principle of least privilege
# groups: www-data allows access to web server sockets/files
- name: Create application user
  user:
    name: anihpj           # Dedicated user for our app
    shell: /bin/bash       # Can SSH in for debugging
    groups: www-data       # Access nginx sockets and static files
    append: yes            # Add to group, don't replace existing groups
    create_home: yes       # Needed for SSH, pip cache, etc.

# STEP 6: SSH key deployment
# WHY: Password auth is disabled; only key-based access allowed
# loop: Add all keys from ssh_authorized_keys variable
- name: Set up SSH authorized keys
  authorized_key:
    user: anihpj
    key: "{{ item }}"
  loop: "{{ ssh_authorized_keys }}"   # Defined in group_vars/all.yml

# STEP 7: Kernel tuning for production workloads
# WHY: Default Linux settings are for desktops, not servers
# These sysctl settings optimize for high network throughput
- name: Configure sysctl for performance
  sysctl:
    name: "{{ item.key }}"
    value: "{{ item.value }}"
    state: present
    reload: yes
  loop:
    - { key: 'net.core.somaxconn', value: '65535' }
    - { key: 'net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog', value: '65535' }
    - { key: 'vm.swappiness', value: '10' }

- name: Configure log rotation
  template:
    src: logrotate.conf.j2
    dest: /etc/logrotate.d/anihpj
    mode: '0644'
📜 ansible/roles/security/tasks/main.yml 
# Security hardening role
---
- name: Disable root SSH login
  lineinfile:
    path: /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    regexp: '^PermitRootLogin'
    line: 'PermitRootLogin no'
  notify: restart sshd

- name: Disable password authentication
  lineinfile:
    path: /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    regexp: '^PasswordAuthentication'
    line: 'PasswordAuthentication no'
  notify: restart sshd

- name: Configure UFW firewall
  ufw:
    rule: allow
    port: "{{ item }}"
    proto: tcp
  loop:
    - '22'
    - '80'
    - '443'

- name: Enable UFW
  ufw:
    state: enabled
    policy: deny
    direction: incoming

- name: Install and configure fail2ban
  apt:
    name: fail2ban
    state: present

- name: Configure fail2ban for SSH
  template:
    src: fail2ban-jail.local.j2
    dest: /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
  notify: restart fail2ban

- name: Enable automatic security updates
  apt:
    name: unattended-upgrades
    state: present

- name: Configure unattended upgrades
  template:
    src: 50unattended-upgrades.j2
    dest: /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades
▶️ Execute Configuration Automation
# Run Ansible playbook
cd ansible

# Check what would change (dry run)
ansible-playbook -i inventory/production.yml playbooks/site.yml --check --diff

# Apply configuration
ansible-playbook -i inventory/production.yml playbooks/site.yml

# Run just security hardening
ansible-playbook -i inventory/production.yml playbooks/site.yml --tags security

Example 3: Deployment Automation (ArgoCD + Helm) Scenario: GitOps-based continuous deployment to Kubernetes
📁 Project Context Files
📄 project-structure 
anihpj/
├── src/                         ← Application code
├── Dockerfile
├── helm/
│   └── anihpj/
│       ├── Chart.yaml
│       ├── values.yaml
│       ├── values-staging.yaml
│       └── values-production.yaml
├── argocd/
│   ├── application.yaml         ← ArgoCD Application
│   └── project.yaml
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── ci.yaml              ← Build only, ArgoCD deploys
🐳 Dockerfile 
FROM python:3.11-slim

ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "4", "anihpj.wsgi"]
⎈ helm/anihpj/values-production.yaml 
# Production values
replicaCount: 5

image:
  repository: registry.example.com/anihpj
  tag: ""  # Set by ArgoCD Image Updater
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

resources:
  requests:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
  limits:
    memory: "1Gi"
    cpu: "1000m"

autoscaling:
  enabled: true
  minReplicas: 5
  maxReplicas: 20
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70

ingress:
  enabled: true
  className: nginx
  hosts:
    - host: anihpj.com
      paths:
        - path: /
          pathType: Prefix
  tls:
    - secretName: anihpj-tls
      hosts:
        - anihpj.com

env:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: anihpj.settings.production
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: anihpj.com
🔄 argocd/application.yaml Deployment Automation 
# Deployment Automation - GitOps with ArgoCD
# This DEPLOYS application code to running infrastructure

apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  namespace: argocd
  # Enable automatic image updates
  annotations:
    argocd-image-updater.argoproj.io/image-list: app=registry.example.com/anihpj
    argocd-image-updater.argoproj.io/app.update-strategy: semver
    argocd-image-updater.argoproj.io/write-back-method: git
spec:
  project: anihpj
  
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/anihpj-org/anihpj.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: helm/anihpj
    helm:
      valueFiles:
        - values.yaml
        - values-production.yaml
  
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true        # Remove resources not in git
      selfHeal: true     # Fix drift automatically
      allowEmpty: false
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
      - PrunePropagationPolicy=foreground
    retry:
      limit: 5
      backoff:
        duration: 5s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 3m

  # Health checks
  ignoreDifferences:
    - group: apps
      kind: Deployment
      jsonPointers:
        - /spec/replicas  # Ignore HPA-managed replicas

---
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AppProject
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  description: ANIHPJ Django Application
  
  sourceRepos:
    - https://github.com/anihpj-org/*
  
  destinations:
    - namespace: production
      server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    - namespace: staging
      server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
  
  clusterResourceWhitelist:
    - group: ''
      kind: Namespace
  
  namespaceResourceWhitelist:
    - group: '*'
      kind: '*'
⚙️ .github/workflows/ci.yaml 
# CI only - ArgoCD handles deployment
name: CI Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python manage.py test

  build:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
      
      - name: Login to Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: registry.example.com
          username: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USER }}
          password: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }}
      
      - name: Build and Push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          push: true
          tags: |
            registry.example.com/anihpj:${{ github.sha }}
            registry.example.com/anihpj:latest
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

      # ArgoCD Image Updater detects new image and deploys automatically!
      # No manual deployment step needed
▶️ Deployment Flow
# Developer pushes code
git push origin main

# CI Pipeline runs automatically:
# 1. Tests pass
# 2. Docker image built and pushed

# ArgoCD detects new image (polling every 2 minutes):
argocd app get anihpj-production

# Manual sync if needed:
argocd app sync anihpj-production

# Check deployment status:
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n production

Deployment automation: Push code → Image builds → ArgoCD deploys → Zero manual steps!

🐚 scripts/daily/backup-database.sh Operational Automation 
#!/bin/bash
# backup-database.sh - Daily database backup
# Triggered by Kubernetes CronJob at 2 AM UTC

set -euo pipefail

BACKUP_BUCKET="anihpj-backups"
DATABASE_URL="${DATABASE_URL}"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
BACKUP_FILE="anihpj-${DATE}.sql.gz"

log() {
    echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1"
}

log "Starting database backup..."

# Create backup
pg_dump "$DATABASE_URL" | gzip > "/tmp/$BACKUP_FILE"

# Upload to S3
aws s3 cp "/tmp/$BACKUP_FILE" "s3://$BACKUP_BUCKET/daily/$BACKUP_FILE"

# Cleanup local file
rm "/tmp/$BACKUP_FILE"

# Verify backup exists
aws s3 ls "s3://$BACKUP_BUCKET/daily/$BACKUP_FILE"

# Delete backups older than 30 days
aws s3 ls "s3://$BACKUP_BUCKET/daily/" | while read -r line; do
    backup_date=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}')
    if [[ "$backup_date" < $(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d) ]]; then
        file=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $4}')
        log "Deleting old backup: $file"
        aws s3 rm "s3://$BACKUP_BUCKET/daily/$file"
    fi
done

# Send success notification
curl -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK" \
    -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
    -d '{"text": "✅ Database backup completed: '"$BACKUP_FILE"'"}'

log "Backup complete!"
🐍 scripts/daily/health-report.py 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
health-report.py - Daily infrastructure health report
Posts summary to Slack every morning
"""
import boto3
import requests
from kubernetes import client, config
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import os

SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ['SLACK_WEBHOOK']

class HealthReporter:
    def __init__(self):
        config.load_incluster_config()
        self.k8s = client.CoreV1Api()
        self.cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch')
    
    def get_pod_health(self) -> dict:
        """Check Kubernetes pod status"""
        pods = self.k8s.list_namespaced_pod('production')
        
        total = len(pods.items)
        running = sum(1 for p in pods.items if p.status.phase == 'Running')
        restarts = sum(
            sum(c.restart_count for c in p.status.container_statuses or [])
            for p in pods.items
        )
        
        return {
            'total': total,
            'running': running,
            'unhealthy': total - running,
            'restarts_24h': restarts
        }
    
    def get_error_rate(self) -> float:
        """Get 5xx error rate from CloudWatch"""
        response = self.cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics(
            Namespace='AWS/ApplicationELB',
            MetricName='HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count',
            Dimensions=[{'Name': 'LoadBalancer', 'Value': 'app/anihpj-alb/xxx'}],
            StartTime=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=24),
            EndTime=datetime.utcnow(),
            Period=86400,
            Statistics=['Sum']
        )
        return response['Datapoints'][0]['Sum'] if response['Datapoints'] else 0
    
    def get_database_metrics(self) -> dict:
        """Get RDS metrics"""
        cpu = self.cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics(
            Namespace='AWS/RDS',
            MetricName='CPUUtilization',
            Dimensions=[{'Name': 'DBInstanceIdentifier', 'Value': 'anihpj-production'}],
            StartTime=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=24),
            EndTime=datetime.utcnow(),
            Period=86400,
            Statistics=['Average', 'Maximum']
        )
        return {
            'cpu_avg': cpu['Datapoints'][0]['Average'] if cpu['Datapoints'] else 0,
            'cpu_max': cpu['Datapoints'][0]['Maximum'] if cpu['Datapoints'] else 0
        }
    
    def generate_report(self) -> str:
        """Generate Slack-formatted report"""
        pods = self.get_pod_health()
        errors = self.get_error_rate()
        db = self.get_database_metrics()
        
        status = "✅" if pods['unhealthy'] == 0 and errors < 100 else "⚠️"
        
        report = f"""
*{status} Daily Health Report - {datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}*

*Kubernetes:*
• Pods: {pods['running']}/{pods['total']} running
• Unhealthy: {pods['unhealthy']}
• Restarts (24h): {pods['restarts_24h']}

*Application:*
• 5xx Errors (24h): {int(errors)}
• Error Rate: {'🔴 High' if errors > 100 else '🟢 Normal'}

*Database:*
• CPU Avg: {db['cpu_avg']:.1f}%
• CPU Max: {db['cpu_max']:.1f}%
• Status: {'🔴 High Load' if db['cpu_max'] > 80 else '🟢 Normal'}
        """
        return report
    
    def send_report(self):
        """Post report to Slack"""
        report = self.generate_report()
        requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={'text': report})


if __name__ == '__main__':
    reporter = HealthReporter()
    reporter.send_report()
🐍 lambda/auto-healer/handler.py Event-Driven Ops 
"""
auto-healer - Lambda function triggered by CloudWatch alarms
Automatically remediates common issues
"""
import boto3
import json
import os
from kubernetes import client, config

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """
    Triggered by SNS when CloudWatch alarm fires
    Automatically tries to fix common issues
    """
    # Parse alarm details
    message = json.loads(event['Records'][0]['Sns']['Message'])
    alarm_name = message['AlarmName']
    
    print(f"Processing alarm: {alarm_name}")
    
    # Route to appropriate handler
    handlers = {
        'HighCPU-Web': handle_high_cpu,
        'HighMemory-Web': handle_high_memory,
        'UnhealthyHosts': handle_unhealthy_hosts,
        'HighDBConnections': handle_db_connections,
    }
    
    if alarm_name in handlers:
        result = handlers[alarm_name](message)
        notify_slack(f"🔧 Auto-healer executed for {alarm_name}: {result}")
        return result
    else:
        notify_slack(f"⚠️ Unknown alarm {alarm_name} - manual intervention required")
        return {'status': 'unknown_alarm'}


def handle_high_cpu(message):
    """Scale up pods when CPU is high"""
    config.load_incluster_config()
    apps_v1 = client.AppsV1Api()
    
    # Get current replicas
    deployment = apps_v1.read_namespaced_deployment('anihpj', 'production')
    current = deployment.spec.replicas
    
    # Scale up by 50%
    new_count = min(current + max(2, current // 2), 20)  # Max 20 pods
    
    deployment.spec.replicas = new_count
    apps_v1.patch_namespaced_deployment('anihpj', 'production', deployment)
    
    return {
        'action': 'scale_up',
        'previous': current,
        'new': new_count
    }


def handle_high_memory(message):
    """Restart pods with highest memory usage"""
    config.load_incluster_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    
    # Get pods sorted by memory
    pods = v1.list_namespaced_pod('production', label_selector='app=anihpj')
    
    # Delete oldest pod (likely has memory leaks)
    oldest = min(pods.items, key=lambda p: p.metadata.creation_timestamp)
    v1.delete_namespaced_pod(oldest.metadata.name, 'production')
    
    return {
        'action': 'restart_pod',
        'pod': oldest.metadata.name
    }


def handle_unhealthy_hosts(message):
    """Replace unhealthy pods"""
    config.load_incluster_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    
    pods = v1.list_namespaced_pod('production', label_selector='app=anihpj')
    
    deleted = []
    for pod in pods.items:
        if pod.status.phase != 'Running':
            v1.delete_namespaced_pod(pod.metadata.name, 'production')
            deleted.append(pod.metadata.name)
    
    return {
        'action': 'replace_unhealthy',
        'deleted': deleted
    }


def handle_db_connections(message):
    """Scale down if DB connections exhausted"""
    config.load_incluster_config()
    apps_v1 = client.AppsV1Api()
    
    deployment = apps_v1.read_namespaced_deployment('anihpj', 'production')
    current = deployment.spec.replicas
    
    # Scale down to reduce connections
    new_count = max(3, current - 2)  # Min 3 pods
    
    deployment.spec.replicas = new_count
    apps_v1.patch_namespaced_deployment('anihpj', 'production', deployment)
    
    return {
        'action': 'scale_down',
        'reason': 'db_connection_limit',
        'previous': current,
        'new': new_count
    }


def notify_slack(message):
    """Send notification to Slack"""
    import urllib.request
    webhook = os.environ['SLACK_WEBHOOK']
    data = json.dumps({'text': message}).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(webhook, data=data, headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'})
    urllib.request.urlopen(req)
☸️ k8s/cronjobs.yaml 
# Scheduled operational tasks
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: daily-backup
  namespace: ops
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # 2 AM UTC
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: registry.example.com/ops-scripts:latest
            command: ["/scripts/daily/backup-database.sh"]
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: ops-secrets
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: daily-health-report
  namespace: ops
spec:
  schedule: "0 8 * * *"  # 8 AM UTC (morning report)
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: reporter
            image: registry.example.com/ops-scripts:latest
            command: ["python", "/scripts/daily/health-report.py"]
            envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: ops-secrets
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: log-cleanup
  namespace: ops
spec:
  schedule: "0 4 * * *"  # 4 AM UTC
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: cleanup
            image: registry.example.com/ops-scripts:latest
            command: ["/scripts/daily/cleanup-logs.sh"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
