Operational automation handles day-to-day tasks WITHOUT human intervention!
Example 5: All Four Types Working Together Scenario: Complete automation lifecycle for a new feature deployment
📁 End-to-End Automation Flow
📋 automation-flow.md 
# Complete Automation Flow: New Feature to Production

## The Scenario
Developer needs to add a new API endpoint that requires:
- New database table
- More memory for pods
- Caching with Redis

## How Each Automation Type Plays a Role:

### 1️⃣ INFRASTRUCTURE AUTOMATION (Terraform)
Developer updates terraform to increase pod memory limits:

```hcl
# terraform/main.tf
resource "helm_release" "anihpj" {
  # ...
  set {
    name  = "resources.limits.memory"
    value = "1Gi"  # Increased from 512Mi
  }
}
```
Pull Request → Review → Merge → Terraform applies automatically

### 2️⃣ CONFIGURATION AUTOMATION (Helm values)
Update configuration for the new feature:

```yaml
# helm/anihpj/values-production.yaml
features:
  newApiEndpoint: true
  cacheEnabled: true
env:
  REDIS_URL: "redis://anihpj-redis:6379/0"
  FEATURE_FLAG_NEW_API: "enabled"
```

### 3️⃣ DEPLOYMENT AUTOMATION (ArgoCD + GitHub Actions)
Push code changes:

```python
# apps/api/views.py
class NewEndpointView(APIView):
    @cache_response(60 * 15)  # 15 min cache
    def get(self, request):
        return Response(data)
```

→ CI runs tests
→ Docker image built
→ ArgoCD detects new image
→ Rolling deployment executed
→ Zero downtime

### 4️⃣ OPERATIONAL AUTOMATION (Scripts + Lambda)
Post-deployment, operational automation kicks in:

- Health report confirms new pods are healthy
- Auto-healer monitors for issues
- Backup job includes new table
- Cost report tracks resource changes

## Timeline:

09:00 - Developer opens PR with new feature
09:15 - CI tests pass, PR approved
09:20 - Merge to main
09:22 - Docker image built (CI)
09:25 - ArgoCD syncs new image
09:30 - Rolling deployment complete
09:35 - Smoke tests pass
09:40 - Monitoring confirms healthy
10:00 - Feature live for users

## What Ran Automatically:
- Infrastructure: Terraform apply (via Atlantis)
- Configuration: Helm values applied via ArgoCD
- Deployment: ArgoCD sync + rolling update
- Operational: Health checks, monitoring setup

## Human Touch Points:
- Write code ✍️
- Review PR 👀
- Approve deployment 👍
- Everything else: AUTOMATED! 🤖
💡 Key Takeaways
Four Types of Automation Summary:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ INFRASTRUCTURE AUTOMATION                                        │
│ "Create the foundation"                                         │
│ Tools: Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation                        │
│ When: Environment setup, scaling infrastructure                 │
│ Frequency: Weekly/Monthly                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CONFIGURATION AUTOMATION                                         │
│ "Set up the foundation correctly"                               │
│ Tools: Ansible, Chef, Puppet, Helm                              │
│ When: Initial setup, drift correction, updates                  │
│ Frequency: On-demand, scheduled drift checks                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ DEPLOYMENT AUTOMATION                                            │
│ "Put code on the foundation"                                    │
│ Tools: ArgoCD, Spinnaker, Jenkins, GitHub Actions               │
│ When: Every code change to main branch                          │
│ Frequency: Multiple times per day                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ OPERATIONAL AUTOMATION                                           │
│ "Keep everything running"                                       │
│ Tools: Scripts, Lambda, CronJobs, PagerDuty                     │
│ When: Continuously, event-driven                                │
│ Frequency: 24/7                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Each type builds on the others:
- Can't configure what doesn't exist (needs infrastructure first)
- Can't deploy to unconfigured systems
- Can't operate what isn't deployed

Master ALL FOUR to be an effective DevOps engineer!

1.5 Choosing the Right Tool for the Task

Decision Making High Beginner

A practical framework for selecting the best scripting language and tool for each DevOps task

Comprehensive Overview

One of the most common mistakes DevOps engineers make is using the wrong tool for the job. Just because you know Python doesn't mean every problem needs a Python solution. Understanding tool strengths helps you work more efficiently and produce better solutions.

Tool Best For Avoid For Learning Curve
Bash System commands, file ops, glue scripts Complex logic, data processing, APIs Low
Python APIs, data processing, complex logic Simple shell tasks, one-liners Medium
PowerShell Windows admin, Azure, AD management Linux systems, cross-platform tools Medium
Ansible Config management, multi-server ops One-off scripts, data processing Medium
Example 1: When Bash is the Right Choice Scenario: Tasks that Bash handles better than Python or other languages
📁 Bash is Perfect For
✅ bash-ideal-tasks.md 
# When to Choose Bash

## ✅ USE BASH WHEN:

1. Chaining Unix commands (pipes, redirects)
2. Simple file operations (copy, move, find)
3. Quick one-liners
4. CI/CD pipeline steps
5. Container entrypoints
6. System initialization scripts
7. Cron job wrappers
8. Glue code between other tools
🐚 examples/bash-wins.sh Bash Excels Here 
#!/bin/bash
# ============================================================================
# WHEN BASH IS THE RIGHT CHOICE
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: Examples where Bash beats Python/other languages
# 
# RULE OF THUMB:
# - If it's mostly calling other programs → Bash
# - If it's mostly data processing/logic → Python
# - If you're chaining Unix commands → Bash will be 10x shorter
# ============================================================================

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. PIPELINE PROCESSING - Bash's superpower (pipes and redirects)
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Designed for chaining commands. Python would need 20+ lines!
# Find large log files, sort by size, show top 10
find /var/log -name "*.log" -exec du -h {} \; | sort -rh | head -10
# BREAKDOWN:
#   find: Recursively search directory
#   -exec: Run command on each result
#   du -h: Human-readable sizes
#   | sort -rh: Pipe to sort (reverse, human-numeric)
#   | head -10: Pipe to show first 10 lines

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. QUICK FILE OPERATIONS - One-liners for file tasks
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: find + exec is incredibly powerful and concise
# Backup all config files modified today
find /etc -name "*.conf" -mtime 0 -exec cp {} /backup/ \;
# -mtime 0 means "modified within last 24 hours"

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. TEXT STREAM PROCESSING - awk + sort + uniq combo
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Unix text tools are blazing fast for log analysis
# Extract IPs that made >100 requests (detect potential abuse)
awk '{print $1}' /var/log/nginx/access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | awk '$1 > 100'
# BREAKDOWN:
#   awk '{print $1}': First column (IP address)
#   sort: Required before uniq (uniq only works on adjacent lines)
#   uniq -c: Count occurrences
#   sort -rn: Sort by count (descending)
#   awk '$1 > 100': Filter to counts > 100

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. MULTI-SERVER COMMANDS - SSH loops
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Simple loop over server list, no dependencies needed
for server in $(cat servers.txt); do
    echo "=== $server ==="
    ssh $server "df -h | grep -E '^/dev'"   # Disk usage on each server
done

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. CONTAINER ENTRYPOINT - Waiting for dependencies
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Perfect for "wait then exec" patterns
until pg_isready -h $DB_HOST -p 5432; do
    echo "Waiting for database..."
    sleep 2
done
exec gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application   # exec replaces shell with app process

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. CI/CD STEPS - Quick deployment commands
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Direct command execution, minimal overhead
git pull origin main
docker build -t app:$GITHUB_SHA .      # Tag with commit SHA for traceability
docker push app:$GITHUB_SHA
kubectl set image deployment/app app=app:$GITHUB_SHA

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 7. QUICK DIAGNOSTICS - One-liners for troubleshooting
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY BASH: Fastest path from "what's wrong" to "answer"
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10         # Top 10 memory consumers
🐳 Dockerfile 
# Bash entrypoint is common and appropriate
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

# Bash entrypoint script - simple and effective
COPY docker-entrypoint.sh /
RUN chmod +x /docker-entrypoint.sh

ENTRYPOINT ["/docker-entrypoint.sh"]
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi"]
🐚 docker-entrypoint.sh 
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Wait for database
echo "Waiting for database..."
while ! pg_isready -h "$DB_HOST" -p "${DB_PORT:-5432}" -q; do
    sleep 1
done
echo "Database is ready!"

# Run migrations
echo "Running migrations..."
python manage.py migrate --noinput

# Collect static files
if [ "$COLLECT_STATIC" = "true" ]; then
    python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
fi

# Execute the main command
exec "$@"
▶️ Real-World Bash Usage
# These tasks are FASTER to write in Bash than Python

# Quick health check
curl -sf http://localhost:8000/health/ && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"

# Rotate logs
find /var/log/app -name "*.log" -mtime +7 -delete

# Sync files to S3
aws s3 sync ./static s3://bucket/static --delete

# Restart failing containers
docker ps -q --filter "status=exited" | xargs -r docker restart
Example 2: When Python is the Right Choice Scenario: Tasks that Python handles better than Bash
📁 Python is Perfect For
✅ python-ideal-tasks.md 
# When to Choose Python

## ✅ USE PYTHON WHEN:

1. Working with APIs (REST, GraphQL)
2. Parsing JSON/YAML/XML
3. Complex data processing
4. Error handling is critical
5. Need external libraries
6. Building CLI tools for teams
7. Cross-platform requirements
8. Logic complexity > 50 lines
9. Need unit tests
10. Working with databases directly
🐍 examples/python-wins.py Python Excels Here 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
============================================================================
WHEN PYTHON IS THE RIGHT CHOICE
============================================================================
PURPOSE: Examples where Python beats Bash/other languages

RULE OF THUMB:
- If it involves APIs → Python (requests library)
- If it involves JSON/YAML → Python (built-in support)
- If it's complex logic → Python (readable, testable)
- If you need error handling → Python (try/except)
- If it needs to be cross-platform → Python

WHY NOT BASH?
- Error handling is painful in Bash
- JSON parsing in Bash = sed/jq gymnastics
- Complex logic = unreadable nested if/loops
- No data structures (just strings and arrays)
============================================================================
"""
import boto3
import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# =========================================================================
# EXAMPLE 1: API INTERACTION - Python's bread and butter
# =========================================================================
# WHY PYTHON: Clean HTTP clients, JSON parsing, data transformation
# Bash equivalent would need curl + jq + sed + multiple temp files
def get_github_prs():
    """
    Fetch and process GitHub PRs to find stale ones.
    
    This combines:
    - HTTP request with auth headers
    - JSON parsing
    - List comprehension with filtering
    - Date arithmetic
    
    Bash would be ~100 lines of curl + jq + awk gymnastics!
    """
    # requests library makes HTTP beautiful
    response = requests.get(
        'https://api.github.com/repos/anihpj/anihpj/pulls',
        headers={'Authorization': f'token {GITHUB_TOKEN}'}  # Auth header
    )
    
    prs = response.json()  # One-liner JSON parsing (vs jq in Bash)
    
    # List comprehension - powerful data transformation
    # This would be multiple loops and temp variables in Bash
    stale_prs = [
        {
            'title': pr['title'],
            'author': pr['user']['login'],
            'days_old': (datetime.now() - datetime.fromisoformat(
                pr['created_at'].replace('Z', '+00:00')
            )).days
        }
        for pr in prs
        if pr['draft'] is False  # Filter out drafts
        and (datetime.now() - datetime.fromisoformat(
            pr['updated_at'].replace('Z', '+00:00')
        )).days > 7  # Only PRs stale > 7 days
    ]
    
    return stale_prs

# =========================================================================
# EXAMPLE 2: AWS SDK OPERATIONS - boto3 is amazing
# =========================================================================
# WHY PYTHON: boto3 handles auth, pagination, retries automatically
# AWS CLI in Bash = string parsing nightmare
def get_expensive_resources():
    """
    Find underutilized EC2 instances by checking CPU metrics.
    
    This combines:
    - EC2 API calls
    - CloudWatch metrics
    - Pagination handling (boto3 does this!)
    - Complex filtering logic
    
    Cost optimization script that pays for itself!
    """
    # boto3 clients auto-handle credentials from env/IAM roles
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
    cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch')
    
    # Describe instances returns paginated results - boto3 handles it
    instances = ec2.describe_instances(
        Filters=[{'Name': 'instance-state-name', 'Values': ['running']}]
    )
    
    underutilized = []
    for reservation in instances['Reservations']:
        for instance in reservation['Instances']:
            # Get average CPU over last 7 days
            metrics = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics(
                Namespace='AWS/EC2',
                MetricName='CPUUtilization',
                Dimensions=[{'Name': 'InstanceId', 'Value': instance['InstanceId']}],
                StartTime=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=7),
                EndTime=datetime.utcnow(),
                Period=86400,  # Daily average
                Statistics=['Average']
            )
            
            avg_cpu = sum(d['Average'] for d in metrics['Datapoints']) / len(metrics['Datapoints']) if metrics['Datapoints'] else 0
            
            if avg_cpu < 10:  # Less than 10% CPU
                underutilized.append({
                    'instance_id': instance['InstanceId'],
                    'type': instance['InstanceType'],
                    'avg_cpu': round(avg_cpu, 2)
                })
    
    return underutilized

# 3. COMPLEX DATA PROCESSING
def analyze_logs(log_file: str) -> dict:
    """Parse and analyze application logs"""
    import re
    from collections import defaultdict
    
    errors = defaultdict(list)
    response_times = []
    
    pattern = re.compile(
        r'(?P<timestamp>\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}) '
        r'(?P<level>\w+) '
        r'(?P<message>.*?) '
        r'response_time=(?P<time>\d+)ms'
    )
    
    with open(log_file) as f:
        for line in f:
            match = pattern.search(line)
            if match:
                data = match.groupdict()
                response_times.append(int(data['time']))
                if data['level'] == 'ERROR':
                    errors[data['message']].append(data['timestamp'])
    
    return {
        'total_requests': len(response_times),
        'avg_response_time': sum(response_times) / len(response_times) if response_times else 0,
        'p95_response_time': sorted(response_times)[int(len(response_times) * 0.95)] if response_times else 0,
        'error_summary': {msg: len(times) for msg, times in errors.items()}
    }

# 4. DATABASE OPERATIONS
def generate_report():
    """Generate report from database"""
    import psycopg2
    from psycopg2.extras import RealDictCursor
    
    conn = psycopg2.connect(DATABASE_URL)
    
    with conn.cursor(cursor_factory=RealDictCursor) as cur:
        cur.execute("""
            SELECT 
                date_trunc('day', created_at) as day,
                COUNT(*) as signups,
                COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_paid) as paid_signups
            FROM users
            WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
            GROUP BY 1
            ORDER BY 1
        """)
        
        return cur.fetchall()

# 5. CLI TOOL WITH PROPER ARGUMENTS
import click

@click.command()
@click.option('--environment', '-e', type=click.Choice(['staging', 'production']))
@click.option('--dry-run', is_flag=True, help='Show what would be done')
@click.option('--verbose', '-v', count=True, help='Increase verbosity')
def deploy(environment, dry_run, verbose):
    """Deploy application to specified environment"""
    if verbose > 0:
        click.echo(f"Deploying to {environment}")
    
    if dry_run:
        click.echo("DRY RUN - no changes will be made")
        return
    
    # Actual deployment logic
    pass
⚠️ examples/dont-use-bash.sh DON'T DO THIS 
#!/bin/bash
# BAD: Complex API interaction in Bash - use Python instead!

# This is painful and error-prone
RESPONSE=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: token $TOKEN" \
    "https://api.github.com/repos/anihpj/anihpj/pulls")

# JSON parsing in bash is a nightmare
echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.[] | select(.draft == false) | 
    select((now - (.updated_at | fromdateiso8601)) / 86400 > 7) |
    {title: .title, author: .user.login}' | \
    while read -r line; do
        # Error handling? Good luck!
        # Complex transformations? Pain!
        # Testing? Impossible!
        echo "$line"
    done

# This Bash script took 20 minutes to write
# The Python version took 5 minutes
# The Python version is testable, readable, maintainable
# USE PYTHON FOR API WORK!
✔️ Python Choice Checklist
# When you see these, reach for Python:

☐ Need to parse JSON/YAML responses
☐ Making HTTP API calls
☐ Complex conditional logic
☐ Error handling is important
☐ Need to write unit tests
☐ Data transformation/aggregation
☐ Working with dates/times
☐ Need external libraries (boto3, requests, etc.)
☐ Script will be maintained long-term
☐ Building a tool others will use
Example 3: Decision Flowchart - Which Tool to Use Scenario: A practical decision tree for everyday DevOps tasks
📁 Tool Selection Flowchart
📋 decision-flowchart.md 
# DevOps Tool Selection Flowchart

```
                     START
                       │
                       ▼
           ┌───────────────────────┐
           │  Is it a one-liner?   │
           └───────────────────────┘
                  │           │
                 YES          NO
                  │           │
                  ▼           ▼
            ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐
            │  BASH   │    │  Working with APIs?  │
            └─────────┘    └──────────────────────┘
                                │           │
                               YES          NO
                                │           │
                                ▼           ▼
                          ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐
                          │ PYTHON  │    │ Windows/Azure focus? │
                          └─────────┘    └──────────────────────┘
                                              │           │
                                             YES          NO
                                              │           │
                                              ▼           ▼
                                        ┌───────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐
                                        │POWERSHELL │  │ Need CLI tool for    │
                                        └───────────┘  │ team distribution?   │
                                                       └──────────────────────┘
                                                            │           │
                                                           YES          NO
                                                            │           │
                                                            ▼           ▼
                                                      ┌─────────┐    ┌───────────┐
                                                      │ PYTHON  │    │    Is it  │
                                                      │(+Click) │    │  < 50     │
                                                      └─────────┘    │   lines?  │
                                                                     └───────────┘
                                                                       │       │
                                                                      YES      NO
                                                                       │       │
                                                                       ▼       ▼
                                                                   ┌─────┐ ┌───────┐
                                                                   │BASH │ │PYTHON │
                                                                   └─────┘ └───────┘
```

# Quick Reference Table

| Task                           | Tool       | Why                              |
|--------------------------------|------------|----------------------------------|
| Find files, grep logs          | Bash       | Built for this                   |
| Call REST API                  | Python     | requests library                 |
| Process JSON data              | Python     | Native dict support              |
| Manage Windows servers         | PowerShell | .NET integration                 |
| Build CLI for team             | Python     | Click/Typer + PyInstaller        |
| CI/CD pipeline step            | Bash       | Simple, universally available    |
| Kubernetes operations          | Python     | kubernetes-client library        |
| AWS automation                 | Python     | boto3 is excellent               |
| Quick server diagnostics       | Bash       | One-liners, pipes                |
| Database migrations            | Python     | ORM/SQL libraries                |
📊 tool-comparison-matrix.yaml 
# Comprehensive tool comparison

bash:
  strengths:
    - File system operations
    - Piping commands together
    - Text processing (grep, sed, awk)
    - Quick one-liners
    - Container entrypoints
    - CI/CD steps
  weaknesses:
    - Error handling
    - Complex logic
    - API interactions
    - Data structures (arrays, maps)
    - Cross-platform
  ideal_script_size: "1-100 lines"
  learning_time: "1 week"
  
python:
  strengths:
    - API interactions (requests, boto3)
    - Data processing (pandas, json)
    - Complex logic
    - Error handling
    - Testing
    - Large ecosystem
  weaknesses:
    - Startup time
    - Distribution (need Python installed)
    - Simple file operations (overkill)
  ideal_script_size: "50-5000 lines"
  learning_time: "2-4 weeks"

powershell:
  strengths:
    - Windows administration
    - Azure management
    - Active Directory
    - .NET integration
    - Object pipeline
  weaknesses:
    - Linux support (improving)
    - Non-Windows tools
    - Cross-platform scripts
  ideal_script_size: "10-500 lines"
  learning_time: "1-2 weeks"

ansible:
  strengths:
    - Agentless
    - Multi-server orchestration
    - Idempotent
    - YAML syntax
    - Large module library
  weaknesses:
    - Not for complex logic
    - Performance at scale
    - Learning YAML quirks
    - Not for data processing
  ideal_script_size: "Playbooks: 50-500 lines"
  learning_time: "2-4 weeks"
Example 4: Same Task - Comparison Across Tools Scenario: Check if a service is healthy across all pods
📁 Task: Health Check All Pods
🐚 healthcheck-bash.sh Bash Version 
#!/bin/bash
# Bash version - Simple but limited error handling

NAMESPACE="production"
LABEL="app=anihpj"

echo "Checking health of pods in $NAMESPACE..."

PODS=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l $LABEL -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}')

for POD in $PODS; do
    HEALTH=$(kubectl exec -n $NAMESPACE $POD -- curl -sf http://localhost:8000/health/ 2>/dev/null)
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "✅ $POD: $HEALTH"
    else
        echo "❌ $POD: UNHEALTHY"
    fi
done

# Pros: Quick to write, simple
# Cons: Limited error handling, no structured output
🐍 healthcheck-python.py Python Version 
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Health check all pods - Python version with better structure"""
from kubernetes import client, config
from kubernetes.stream import stream
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List
import json

@dataclass
class PodHealth:
    name: str
    healthy: bool
    response: dict | None
    error: str | None

def check_pod_health(v1: client.CoreV1Api, namespace: str, pod_name: str) -> PodHealth:
    """Check health of a single pod"""
    try:
        exec_command = ['curl', '-sf', 'http://localhost:8000/health/']
        resp = stream(
            v1.connect_get_namespaced_pod_exec,
            pod_name, namespace,
            command=exec_command,
            stderr=True, stdin=False, stdout=True, tty=False
        )
        return PodHealth(
            name=pod_name,
            healthy=True,
            response=json.loads(resp),
            error=None
        )
    except Exception as e:
        return PodHealth(
            name=pod_name,
            healthy=False,
            response=None,
            error=str(e)
        )

def check_all_pods(namespace: str = "production", label: str = "app=anihpj") -> List[PodHealth]:
    """Check health of all pods matching label"""
    config.load_kube_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    
    pods = v1.list_namespaced_pod(namespace, label_selector=label)
    
    results = []
    for pod in pods.items:
        health = check_pod_health(v1, namespace, pod.metadata.name)
        results.append(health)
        
        status = "✅" if health.healthy else "❌"
        print(f"{status} {health.name}: {'OK' if health.healthy else health.error}")
    
    return results

if __name__ == '__main__':
    results = check_all_pods()
    
    # Can easily extend: export to JSON, send to Slack, etc.
    healthy_count = sum(1 for r in results if r.healthy)
    print(f"\nSummary: {healthy_count}/{len(results)} pods healthy")

# Pros: Structured output, error handling, testable, extensible
# Cons: More code, requires kubernetes library
� healthcheck_api.py FastAPI Version 
# Health check as FastAPI microservice - for production monitoring
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from kubernetes import client, config
from typing import Optional
import asyncio

app = FastAPI(title="Pod Health API", version="1.0.0")

class PodHealth(BaseModel):
    name: str
    healthy: bool
    error: Optional[str] = None
    namespace: str = "production"

class HealthSummary(BaseModel):
    total: int
    healthy: int
    unhealthy: int
    pods: list[PodHealth]

# Load k8s config (works in-cluster or local)
try:
    config.load_incluster_config()
except:
    config.load_kube_config()

v1 = client.CoreV1Api()

@app.get("/health/pods", response_model=HealthSummary)
async def check_all_pods(
    namespace: str = "production",
    label_selector: str = "app=anihpj"
):
    """Health check all pods matching label selector"""
    pods = v1.list_namespaced_pod(
        namespace=namespace,
        label_selector=label_selector
    )
    
    results = []
    for pod in pods.items:
        is_healthy = all(
            cs.ready for cs in (pod.status.container_statuses or [])
        )
        results.append(PodHealth(
            name=pod.metadata.name,
            healthy=is_healthy,
            error=None if is_healthy else "Container not ready",
            namespace=namespace
        ))
    
    healthy_count = sum(1 for r in results if r.healthy)
    return HealthSummary(
        total=len(results),
        healthy=healthy_count,
        unhealthy=len(results) - healthy_count,
        pods=results
    )

@app.get("/health")
async def self_health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "service": "pod-health-api"}

# Run: uvicorn healthcheck_api:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080
# Pros: REST API, async, auto-docs, Pydantic validation, production-ready
# Cons: Requires running service (not single-run script)
✔️ Which Version to Use?
# Decision for this health check task:

Quick debugging on your laptop?
→ Use BASH (fast to type, no setup)

Building into CI/CD pipeline?
→ Use BASH (simpler, less dependencies)

Need structured output for monitoring?
→ Use PYTHON (easy JSON handling)

Building a CLI tool for your team?
→ Use PYTHON (Click/Typer + PyInstaller for binary)

# The "right" tool depends on:
# 1. How quickly you need it
# 2. Who else will use it
# 3. How long it needs to be maintained
# 4. What output format you need
Example 5: Anti-Patterns - Wrong Tool for the Job Scenario: Common mistakes DevOps engineers make with tool selection
📁 Common Anti-Patterns
⛔ anti-patterns.md 
# Tool Selection Anti-Patterns

## ❌ ANTI-PATTERN 1: "I'll do everything in Python"

Problem: Using Python for simple shell operations
```python
# BAD: This is overkill
import subprocess
import os

os.chdir('/var/log')
result = subprocess.run(['find', '.', '-name', '*.log', '-mtime', '+7'], 
                       capture_output=True)
files = result.stdout.decode().split('\n')
for f in files:
    os.remove(f)
```

```bash
# GOOD: Just use bash
find /var/log -name "*.log" -mtime +7 -delete
```

## ❌ ANTI-PATTERN 2: "Bash can do everything"

Problem: Complex business logic in bash
```bash
# BAD: This is a nightmare to maintain
RESPONSE=$(curl -s $API_URL)
USER_ID=$(echo $RESPONSE | jq -r '.user.id')
ORDERS=$(curl -s "$API_URL/users/$USER_ID/orders")
TOTAL=$(echo $ORDERS | jq '[.[] | .amount] | add')
# 100 more lines of bash JSON wrangling...
```

```python
# GOOD: Use Python for API work
response = requests.get(f"{API_URL}")
user_id = response.json()['user']['id']
orders = requests.get(f"{API_URL}/users/{user_id}/orders").json()
total = sum(order['amount'] for order in orders)
```

## ❌ ANTI-PATTERN 3: "Let's build a complex CLI for this one-time task"

Problem: Over-engineering
```python
# BAD: 200 lines of Python with Click, rich output, config files
# for a one-time data migration. This took 4 hours to write
```

```python
# GOOD: Quick script that does the job
# This took 30 minutes to write and never runs again
```

## ❌ ANTI-PATTERN 4: "We need cross-platform, so PowerShell"

Problem: PowerShell on Linux has friction
```powershell
# Painful: PowerShell on Linux servers
Invoke-RestMethod -Uri "http://api.example.com" | ConvertFrom-Json
```

```python
# Better: Python works everywhere smoothly
requests.get("http://api.example.com").json()
```
✅ rules-of-thumb.yaml 
# Tool Selection Rules of Thumb

rules:
  - name: "The 10-Line Rule"
    description: "If you can do it in < 10 lines of Bash, use Bash"
    
  - name: "The API Rule"
    description: "If it involves HTTP APIs, use Python"
    
  - name: "The Distribution Rule"
    description: "If others need to run it without setup, use Python + PyInstaller"
    
  - name: "The Windows Rule"
    description: "If it's Windows/Azure centric, use PowerShell"
    
  - name: "The One-Time Rule"
    description: "If you'll only run it once, use the fastest to write"
    
  - name: "The Team Rule"
    description: "If the team knows Python, write it in Python"
    
  - name: "The CI/CD Rule"
    description: "CI/CD steps should be Bash unless complex"

quick_decisions:
  "Parse JSON from API": "Python"
  "Find and delete files": "Bash"
  "Windows server config": "PowerShell"
  "Team CLI tool": "Python (Click/Typer) + PyInstaller"
  "Container entrypoint": "Bash"
  "Cron job wrapper": "Bash"
  "AWS automation": "Python (boto3)"
  "Log analysis": "Bash for simple, Python for complex"
  "Data transformation": "Python"
  "Quick server check": "Bash"
  "Complex deployment logic": "Python"
💡 Key Takeaways
Choosing the Right Tool - Summary:

1. DON'T DEFAULT TO YOUR FAVORITE
   → Each tool has strengths, use them

2. CONSIDER THESE FACTORS:
   → How quickly do I need it?
   → Who else will use/maintain it?
   → What's the input/output?
   → How complex is the logic?

3. QUICK REFERENCE:
   → File operations? BASH
   → API calls? PYTHON
   → Windows admin? POWERSHELL
   → Distributed CLI? PYTHON + PyInstaller
   → Simple + quick? BASH
   → Complex + maintained? PYTHON

4. TIME INVESTMENT GUIDE:
   → One-liner → Bash (30 seconds)
   → Quick script → Bash (5-30 minutes)
   → Moderate tool → Python (1-4 hours)
   → Team CLI → Python + Click (4-8 hours)

5. THE META-RULE:
   → If you're fighting the tool, you picked wrong
   → Switch early, not after 100 lines of pain

1.6 Setting Up Your Automation Environment

Environment Setup Critical Beginner

Complete setup guide for a professional DevOps automation development environment

Comprehensive Overview

A proper automation environment is the foundation of productive DevOps work. This section covers setting up your local machine, cloud access, essential tools, and development workflows that will make you efficient and effective.

Category Essential Tools Purpose
Shell Bash, Zsh, Fish Primary command interface
Languages Python 3.11+, Node.js Scripting & tool development
Containers Docker, kubectl, helm Container & K8s operations
Cloud CLIs aws, az, gcloud Cloud provider interaction
IaC Terraform, Ansible Infrastructure automation
Example 1: Complete Linux/Mac Development Environment Scenario: Setting up a fresh machine for DevOps automation work
📁 Environment Bootstrap Script
🐚 setup-devops-env.sh Bootstrap Script 
#!/bin/bash
# ============================================================================
# DEVOPS ENVIRONMENT SETUP SCRIPT
# ============================================================================
# PURPOSE: One-command setup for complete DevOps development environment
# WHEN TO USE: New laptop, fresh VM, onboarding new team members
#
# WHAT THIS INSTALLS:
# - Shell: Zsh + Oh My Zsh (productivity boost)
# - Python: pyenv + Python 3.11 + essential packages
# - Containers: Docker, kubectl, helm, k9s
# - Cloud CLIs: AWS CLI, Azure CLI
# - IaC Tools: Terraform, Ansible
#
# WHY SCRIPT THIS?
# - Consistency: Everyone has identical setup
# - Speed: 20 minutes vs 2 hours manual
# - Documentation: Script IS the documentation
# - Onboarding: New engineer? Run this script!
# ============================================================================

set -e  # Exit immediately on any error (fail fast)

echo "🚀 Setting up DevOps automation environment..."

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# OS DETECTION - Adapt installation commands to the OS
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY: Different OSes use different package managers
# Mac = Homebrew, Debian/Ubuntu = apt, RHEL/Fedora = dnf
if [[ "$OSTYPE" == "darwin"* ]]; then
    OS="mac"
    PACKAGE_MANAGER="brew"
elif [[ -f /etc/debian_version ]]; then
    OS="debian"
    PACKAGE_MANAGER="apt"
elif [[ -f /etc/redhat-release ]]; then
    OS="rhel"
    PACKAGE_MANAGER="dnf"
fi

echo "📦 Detected OS: $OS"

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. SHELL SETUP (Zsh + Oh My Zsh)
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY ZSH?
# - Better auto-completion than bash
# - Themes and plugins (git status in prompt)
# - Syntax highlighting as you type
# - Auto-suggestions from history
# ROI: 30 minutes of setup → hours saved over time
echo "🐚 Setting up Zsh..."

if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew install zsh zsh-completions     # Zsh and enhanced completions
elif [[ "$PACKAGE_MANAGER" == "apt" ]]; then
    sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y zsh
fi

# Install Oh My Zsh (framework for zsh config) - skip if already exists
if [ ! -d "$HOME/.oh-my-zsh" ]; then
    sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)" "" --unattended
fi

# Install productivity plugins (HUGE time savers)
# zsh-autosuggestions: Suggests commands from history as you type
# zsh-syntax-highlighting: Colors commands (red if invalid)
git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions
git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-~/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. PYTHON SETUP (pyenv for version management)
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY PYENV?
# - System Python is for OS, not your projects
# - Need different Python versions for different projects
# - Clean isolation from system packages
# - Never "sudo pip install" again!
echo "🐍 Setting up Python..."

# Install pyenv (Python version manager - like nvm for Node)
curl https://pyenv.run | bash

# Add pyenv to shell configuration (loads on every terminal)
cat >> ~/.zshrc << 'EOF'
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
command -v pyenv >/dev/null || export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"
eval "$(pyenv init -)"
EOF

# Reload shell config and install Python
source ~/.zshrc
pyenv install 3.11.6      # Install specific version
pyenv global 3.11.6       # Set as default

# Install DevOps Python essentials
pip install --upgrade pip   # Always upgrade pip first
pip install \
    boto3 \                 # AWS SDK - you'll use this constantly
    requests \              # HTTP library - API calls
    pyyaml \                # YAML parsing (Kubernetes, Ansible)
    python-dotenv \         # Load .env files (local development)
    click \                 # CLI framework (build tools)
    rich \                  # Beautiful terminal output (tables, progress)
    kubernetes \            # Kubernetes Python client
    ansible                 # Config management (can also be installed via pipx)

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. CONTAINER TOOLS (Docker, kubectl, helm, k9s)
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WHY THESE TOOLS?
# - Docker: Build and test containers locally
# - kubectl: Interact with Kubernetes clusters
# - helm: Package manager for Kubernetes (like apt for K8s)
# - k9s: Terminal UI for Kubernetes (MASSIVE productivity boost)
echo "🐳 Setting up container tools..."

if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew install --cask docker    # Docker Desktop (includes daemon)
    brew install kubectl helm k9s  # K8s CLI tools
elif [[ "$PACKAGE_MANAGER" == "apt" ]]; then
    # Docker - official install script handles everything
    curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
    sudo usermod -aG docker $USER  # Allow running without sudo
    
    # kubectl - download matching version for cluster compatibility
    curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
    sudo install kubectl /usr/local/bin/
    
    # helm - official install script
    curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash
fi

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. CLOUD CLIs
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
echo "☁️ Setting up cloud CLIs..."

# AWS CLI
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip
sudo ./aws/install
rm -rf aws awscliv2.zip

# Azure CLI
if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew install azure-cli
else
    curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash
fi

# Google Cloud CLI
if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew install --cask google-cloud-sdk
fi

#---------------------------------------------
# 5. IaC TOOLS
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🏗️ Setting up IaC tools..."

# Terraform
if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew tap hashicorp/tap
    brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform
else
    wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg
    echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
    sudo apt update && sudo apt install terraform
fi

# tfenv for Terraform version management
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc

#---------------------------------------------
# 6. USEFUL CLI TOOLS
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🔧 Installing productivity tools..."

if [[ "$OS" == "mac" ]]; then
    brew install \
        jq \           # JSON processor
        yq \           # YAML processor
        fzf \          # Fuzzy finder
        bat \          # Better cat
        ripgrep \      # Better grep
        fd \           # Better find
        tree \         # Directory tree
        watch \        # Watch command
        htop \         # Better top
        tldr           # Better man pages
else
    sudo apt install -y jq watch htop tree
    # Install others from GitHub releases for latest versions
fi

echo "✅ DevOps environment setup complete!"
echo "🔄 Please restart your terminal or run: source ~/.zshrc"
⚙️ .zshrc Shell Config 
# DevOps-optimized .zshrc

# Oh My Zsh configuration
export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"
ZSH_THEME="robbyrussell"
plugins=(
    git
    docker
    docker-compose
    kubectl
    helm
    terraform
    aws
    zsh-autosuggestions
    zsh-syntax-highlighting
)
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# Path setup
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
export PATH="$HOME/.pyenv/bin:$PATH"
export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"

# Python (pyenv)
eval "$(pyenv init -)"

# Kubernetes
export KUBECONFIG="$HOME/.kube/config"
alias k="kubectl"
alias kgp="kubectl get pods"
alias kgs="kubectl get svc"
alias kgd="kubectl get deployments"
alias klog="kubectl logs -f"
alias kexec="kubectl exec -it"

# Docker
alias d="docker"
alias dc="docker-compose"
alias dps="docker ps"
alias dlog="docker logs -f"

# Terraform
alias tf="terraform"
alias tfi="terraform init"
alias tfp="terraform plan"
alias tfa="terraform apply"

# AWS
alias awsid="aws sts get-caller-identity"
alias awsprofile="export AWS_PROFILE"

# Git shortcuts
alias gs="git status"
alias gp="git pull"
alias gpo="git push origin"
alias gcm="git commit -m"

# Useful functions
function kns() { kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=$1; }
function awsenv() { export AWS_PROFILE=$1; aws sts get-caller-identity; }

# FZF configuration
[ -f ~/.fzf.zsh ] && source ~/.fzf.zsh
▶️ Run the Setup
# Make executable and run
chmod +x setup-devops-env.sh
./setup-devops-env.sh

# Restart terminal, then verify
source ~/.zshrc
📤 Verification Output
$ python --version
Python 3.11.6

$ terraform --version

$ kubectl version --client
Client Version: v1.28.3

$ aws --version
aws-cli/2.13.32 Python/3.11.6

$ docker --version
Docker version 24.0.7, build afdd53b
Example 2: Project-Level Environment Setup Scenario: Isolated environment for the anihpj Django project
📁 Project Directory Structure
📁 anihpj/ Directory Tree 
anihpj/
├── .venv/                    # Python virtual environment
├── .envrc                    # direnv auto-activation
├── .env                      # Environment variables
├── .env.example              # Template for .env
├── .python-version           # pyenv version lock
├── .terraform-version        # tfenv version lock
├── requirements/
│   ├── base.txt
│   ├── dev.txt
│   └── prod.txt
├── scripts/
│   ├── setup-local.sh
│   ├── run-tests.sh
│   └── deploy.sh
├── terraform/
│   └── environments/
│       ├── dev/
│       ├── staging/
│       └── production/
├── kubernetes/
│   ├── base/
│   └── overlays/
├── docker-compose.yml
├── Dockerfile
├── Makefile                  # Common commands
└── README.md
⚙️ .envrc direnv auto-load 
# .envrc - Auto-activate environment when entering directory
# Install direnv: brew install direnv

# Auto-activate Python virtualenv
if [ -d ".venv" ]; then
    source .venv/bin/activate
fi

# Load environment variables
if [ -f ".env" ]; then
    dotenv
fi

# Set project-specific variables
export PROJECT_NAME="anihpj"
export ENVIRONMENT="${ENVIRONMENT:-development}"

# Kubernetes context for this project
export KUBECONFIG="$HOME/.kube/anihpj-config"

# AWS profile for this project
export AWS_PROFILE="anihpj-dev"
export AWS_REGION="us-east-1"

# Terraform workspace
export TF_WORKSPACE="${ENVIRONMENT}"

# Path additions
PATH_add scripts
PATH_add .venv/bin
📋 .env.example 
# .env.example - Copy to .env and fill in values
# DO NOT commit .env to git!

# Django
DEBUG=true
SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key-here
ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost,127.0.0.1

# Database
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/anihpj

# Redis
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0

# AWS
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=
AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1
S3_BUCKET=anihpj-dev-assets

# External Services
STRIPE_API_KEY=
SENDGRID_API_KEY=
🐚 scripts/setup-local.sh 
#!/bin/bash
#============================================================
# Local Development Environment Setup
# Usage: ./scripts/setup-local.sh
#============================================================

set -e
cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."
PROJECT_ROOT=$(pwd)

echo "🚀 Setting up anihpj local development environment..."

#---------------------------------------------
# 1. Python Environment
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🐍 Setting up Python environment..."

# Create virtual environment if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d ".venv" ]; then
    python -m venv .venv
fi

# Activate virtual environment
source .venv/bin/activate

# Upgrade pip and install dependencies
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -r requirements/dev.txt

#---------------------------------------------
# 2. Environment Variables
#---------------------------------------------
echo "⚙️ Setting up environment variables..."

if [ ! -f ".env" ]; then
    cp .env.example .env
    echo "📝 Created .env from template - please edit with your values"
fi

#---------------------------------------------
# 3. Docker Services
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🐳 Starting Docker services..."

docker-compose up -d db redis

echo "⏳ Waiting for database to be ready..."
until docker-compose exec -T db pg_isready; do
    sleep 1
done

#---------------------------------------------
# 4. Database Setup
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🗃️ Setting up database..."

python manage.py migrate
python manage.py loaddata fixtures/dev_data.json || true

#---------------------------------------------
# 5. Pre-commit Hooks
#---------------------------------------------
echo "🪝 Setting up pre-commit hooks..."

pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install

#---------------------------------------------
# 6. Final Verification
#---------------------------------------------
echo "✅ Running verification checks..."

python manage.py check
python manage.py test --parallel || echo "⚠️ Some tests failed"

echo ""
echo "=============================================="
echo "✅ Setup complete!"
echo ""
echo "To start working:"
echo "  source .venv/bin/activate"
echo "  python manage.py runserver"
echo ""
echo "Or with Docker:"
echo "  docker-compose up"
echo "=============================================="
📋 Makefile 
# Makefile - Common development commands
# Usage: make <target>

.PHONY: help setup dev test lint deploy clean

# Default target
help:
	@echo "Available commands:"
	@echo "  make setup     - Initial project setup"
	@echo "  make dev       - Start development server"
	@echo "  make test      - Run tests"
	@echo "  make lint      - Run linters"
	@echo "  make deploy    - Deploy to staging"
	@echo "  make clean     - Clean up"

# Setup
setup:
	@./scripts/setup-local.sh

# Development
dev:
	docker-compose up -d db redis
	source .venv/bin/activate && python manage.py runserver

dev-docker:
	docker-compose up --build

# Testing
test:
	source .venv/bin/activate && python manage.py test --parallel

test-cov:
	source .venv/bin/activate && pytest --cov=. --cov-report=html

# Linting
lint:
	source .venv/bin/activate && \
	black --check . && \
	isort --check-only . && \
	flake8 .

lint-fix:
	source .venv/bin/activate && \
	black . && \
	isort .

# Database
migrate:
	source .venv/bin/activate && python manage.py migrate

makemigrations:
	source .venv/bin/activate && python manage.py makemigrations

# Deployment
deploy-staging:
	./scripts/deploy.sh staging

deploy-prod:
	./scripts/deploy.sh production

# Cleanup
clean:
	docker-compose down -v
	find . -type d -name __pycache__ -exec rm -rf {} + 2>/dev/null || true
	find . -type f -name "*.pyc" -delete
	rm -rf .pytest_cache htmlcov .coverage
▶️ Initialize Project Environment
# Clone and setup
git clone git@github.com:company/anihpj.git
cd anihpj

# Run setup
make setup

# Start development
make dev
Example 3: Cloud Access & Authentication Setup Scenario: Configure secure access to AWS, Azure, and Kubernetes
📁 Cloud Authentication Files
☁️ ~/.aws/config AWS Config 
# AWS CLI configuration with multiple profiles

[default]
region = us-east-1
output = json

[profile anihpj-dev]
region = us-east-1
output = json
# Uses SSO for authentication
sso_start_url = https://mycompany.awsapps.com/start
sso_region = us-east-1
sso_account_id = 123456789012
sso_role_name = DeveloperAccess

[profile anihpj-staging]
region = us-east-1
output = json
sso_start_url = https://mycompany.awsapps.com/start
sso_region = us-east-1
sso_account_id = 234567890123
sso_role_name = DeveloperAccess

[profile anihpj-prod]
region = us-east-1
output = json
# Production uses assumed role from staging
source_profile = anihpj-staging
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::345678901234:role/ProductionDeployRole
mfa_serial = arn:aws:iam::234567890123:mfa/myuser
☸️ ~/.kube/config Kubernetes Config 
# Kubernetes configuration for multiple clusters
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: anihpj-dev

clusters:
  - cluster:
      certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi...
      server: https://dev-cluster.eks.amazonaws.com
    name: anihpj-dev-cluster
    
  - cluster:
      certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi...
      server: https://staging-cluster.eks.amazonaws.com
    name: anihpj-staging-cluster
    
  - cluster:
      certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi...
      server: https://prod-cluster.eks.amazonaws.com
    name: anihpj-prod-cluster

contexts:
  - context:
      cluster: anihpj-dev-cluster
      namespace: anihpj
      user: anihpj-dev-user
    name: anihpj-dev
    
  - context:
      cluster: anihpj-staging-cluster
      namespace: anihpj
      user: anihpj-staging-user
    name: anihpj-staging
    
  - context:
      cluster: anihpj-prod-cluster
      namespace: anihpj
      user: anihpj-prod-user
    name: anihpj-prod

users:
  - name: anihpj-dev-user
    user:
      exec:
        apiVersion: client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1
        command: aws
        args:
          - eks
          - get-token
          - --cluster-name
          - anihpj-dev
          - --profile
          - anihpj-dev
🐚 scripts/setup-cloud-access.sh 
#!/bin/bash
#============================================================
# Cloud Access Setup Script
# Sets up AWS, Azure, GCP, and Kubernetes access
#============================================================

set -e

echo "☁️ Setting up cloud access..."

#---------------------------------------------
# AWS SSO Login
#---------------------------------------------
setup_aws() {
    echo "🔐 Setting up AWS access..."
    
    # Login to AWS SSO
    aws sso login --profile anihpj-dev
    
    # Verify access
    aws sts get-caller-identity --profile anihpj-dev
    
    # Update kubeconfig for EKS
    aws eks update-kubeconfig \
        --name anihpj-dev \
        --profile anihpj-dev \
        --alias anihpj-dev
        
    echo "✅ AWS access configured"
}

#---------------------------------------------
# Azure Login
#---------------------------------------------
setup_azure() {
    echo "🔐 Setting up Azure access..."
    
    # Login to Azure
    az login
    
    # Set subscription
    az account set --subscription "anihpj-development"
    
    # Get AKS credentials
    az aks get-credentials \
        --resource-group anihpj-rg \
        --name anihpj-aks \
        --context anihpj-azure
        
    echo "✅ Azure access configured"
}

#---------------------------------------------
# Kubernetes Context Setup
#---------------------------------------------
setup_kubectl() {
    echo "☸️ Setting up Kubernetes contexts..."
    
    # Verify all contexts
    echo "Available contexts:"
    kubectl config get-contexts
    
    # Set default context
    kubectl config use-context anihpj-dev
    
    # Verify access
    kubectl cluster-info
    kubectl get nodes
    
    echo "✅ Kubernetes access configured"
}

#---------------------------------------------
# Docker Registry Auth
#---------------------------------------------
setup_docker_registry() {
    echo "🐳 Setting up Docker registry access..."
    
    # ECR login
    aws ecr get-login-password --profile anihpj-dev | \
        docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
        123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
        
    echo "✅ Docker registry access configured"
}

# Run all setups
case "${1:-all}" in
    aws)      setup_aws ;;
    azure)    setup_azure ;;
    k8s)      setup_kubectl ;;
    docker)   setup_docker_registry ;;
    all)
        setup_aws
        setup_kubectl
        setup_docker_registry
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Usage: $0 [aws|azure|k8s|docker|all]"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

echo ""
echo "✅ Cloud access setup complete!"
▶️ Setup Cloud Access
# Setup all cloud access
./scripts/setup-cloud-access.sh all

# Or setup individually
./scripts/setup-cloud-access.sh aws
./scripts/setup-cloud-access.sh k8s

Example 4: VS Code DevOps Configuration Scenario: Optimize VS Code for DevOps development workflow
📁 VS Code Configuration Files
📦 .vscode/extensions.json 
{
    "recommendations": [
        // Python
        "ms-python.python",
        "ms-python.vscode-pylance",
        "ms-python.black-formatter",
        
        // Docker & Kubernetes
        "ms-azuretools.vscode-docker",
        "ms-kubernetes-tools.vscode-kubernetes-tools",
        
        // Cloud
        "amazonwebservices.aws-toolkit-vscode",
        "ms-vscode.azure-account",
        "ms-azuretools.vscode-azureterraform",
        
        // IaC
        "hashicorp.terraform",
        "redhat.vscode-yaml",
        "redhat.ansible",
        
        // Git
        "eamodio.gitlens",
        "github.copilot",
        
        // Utilities
        "ms-vscode-remote.remote-containers",
        "timonwong.shellcheck",
        "foxundermoon.shell-format",
        "mechatroner.rainbow-csv"
    ]
}

{
    // Python
    "python.defaultInterpreterPath": "${workspaceFolder}/.venv/bin/python",
    "python.formatting.provider": "black",
    "python.linting.enabled": true,
    "python.linting.flake8Enabled": true,
    "[python]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
            "source.organizeImports": true
        }
    },
    
    // Terraform
    "terraform.languageServer": {
        "enabled": true,
        "args": ["serve"]
    },
    "[terraform]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform"
    },
    
    // YAML (Kubernetes, Ansible)
    "yaml.schemas": {
        "kubernetes": "kubernetes/**/*.yaml",
        "https://json.schemastore.org/ansible-playbook": "ansible/**/*.yml"
    },
    "[yaml]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true
    },
    
    // Docker
    "docker.environment": {
        "COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME": "anihpj"
    },
    
    // Shell scripts
    "[shellscript]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.defaultFormatter": "foxundermoon.shell-format"
    },
    "shellcheck.enable": true,
    
    // File associations
    "files.associations": {
        "*.env*": "dotenv",
        "Dockerfile*": "dockerfile",
        "*.yml": "yaml",
        "*.yaml": "yaml"
    },
    
    // Exclude from search
    "files.exclude": {
        "**/__pycache__": true,
        "**/.pytest_cache": true,
        "**/.terraform": true,
        "**/node_modules": true,
        "**/.venv": true
    }
}
