15.1 PowerShell Overview and Use Cases Understanding PowerShell's architecture
⚡ PowerShell Architecture 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    POWERSHELL ECOSYSTEM                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐        │
│    │  Scripts    │    │  Modules    │    │  Console    │        │
│    │   (.ps1)    │    │  (.psm1)    │    │   (Host)    │        │
│    └─────┬───────┘    └─────┬───────┘    └─────┬───────┘        │
│          │                  │                  │                 │
│          └──────────────────┼──────────────────┘                 │
│                             ▼                                    │
│             ┌───────────────────────────────┐                    │
│             │      POWERSHELL ENGINE        │                    │
│             │  ┌─────────┐  ┌────────────┐  │                    │
│             │  │ Parser  │  │ Pipeline   │  │                    │
│             │  └─────────┘  └────────────┘  │                    │
│             │  ┌─────────┐  ┌────────────┐  │                    │
│             │  │Cmdlets  │  │ Providers  │  │                    │
│             │  └─────────┘  └────────────┘  │                    │
│             └───────────────────────────────┘                    │
│                             │                                    │
│                             ▼                                    │
│             ┌───────────────────────────────┐                    │
│             │          .NET RUNTIME         │                    │
│             │  (Full Framework or .NET 7+)  │                    │
│             └───────────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                                  │
│    KEY INSIGHT: Everything in PowerShell is a .NET OBJECT       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📊 PowerShell vs Windows PowerShell 
┌──────────────────────┬─────────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
│ Feature              │ Windows PowerShell  │ PowerShell (Core)   │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┤
│ Version              │ 5.1 (final)         │ 7.x (current)       │
│ Executable           │ powershell.exe      │ pwsh.exe            │
│ Runtime              │ .NET Framework      │ .NET 7+             │
│ Platforms            │ Windows only        │ Win/Mac/Linux       │
│ Active Development   │ Security fixes only │ New features        │
│ Module Compatibility │ All Windows modules │ Most (compatibility)│
│ Recommendation       │ Legacy scripts      │ New development     │
└──────────────────────┴─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┘

Install PowerShell 7+: winget install Microsoft.PowerShell
15.2 Cmdlets and the Verb-Noun Convention Understanding PowerShell's command structure
🔤 Common Approved Verbs 
┌────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Verb       │ Use When                        │ Examples          │
├────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Get        │ Retrieving data                 │ Get-Process       │
│ Set        │ Modifying existing data         │ Set-Content       │
│ New        │ Creating new resources          │ New-Item          │
│ Remove     │ Deleting resources              │ Remove-Item       │
│ Start      │ Initiating operations           │ Start-Service     │
│ Stop       │ Halting operations              │ Stop-Process      │
│ Invoke     │ Performing actions              │ Invoke-Command    │
│ Test       │ Verification/validation         │ Test-Path         │
│ Export     │ Saving to external format       │ Export-Csv        │
│ Import     │ Loading from external format    │ Import-Module     │
│ ConvertTo  │ Converting to a format          │ ConvertTo-Json    │
│ ConvertFrom│ Converting from a format        │ ConvertFrom-Json  │
│ Out        │ Sending output somewhere        │ Out-File          │
│ Write      │ Writing to stream               │ Write-Host        │
│ Read       │ Reading input                   │ Read-Host         │
│ Select     │ Choosing properties/objects     │ Select-Object     │
│ Where      │ Filtering objects               │ Where-Object      │
│ ForEach    │ Iterating over collection       │ ForEach-Object    │
└────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┘

Get full list: Get-Verb | Sort-Object Verb
▶️ Common Cmdlets Quick Reference
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FILE SYSTEM OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Get-ChildItem            # List directory (alias: ls, dir, gci)
Get-Content              # Read file content (alias: cat, gc)
Set-Content              # Write file content (overwrites)
Add-Content              # Append to file
New-Item                 # Create file or directory
Remove-Item              # Delete file/directory (alias: rm, del)
Copy-Item                # Copy file/directory (alias: cp)
Move-Item                # Move/rename (alias: mv)
Test-Path                # Check if path exists
Get-Location             # Current directory (alias: pwd)
Set-Location             # Change directory (alias: cd)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PROCESS AND SERVICE MANAGEMENT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Get-Process              # List processes (alias: ps)
Stop-Process             # Kill process (alias: kill)
Start-Process            # Launch application
Get-Service              # List services
Start-Service            # Start service
Stop-Service             # Stop service
Restart-Service          # Restart service

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OUTPUT AND FORMATTING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Write-Output             # Write to success pipeline (alias: echo)
Write-Host               # Write to console (bypasses pipeline)
Write-Error              # Write to error stream
Write-Warning            # Write warning message
Write-Verbose            # Write verbose message (requires -Verbose)
Format-Table             # Format as table (alias: ft)
Format-List              # Format as list (alias: fl)
Out-File                 # Send output to file
Out-GridView             # GUI grid view (Windows only)
15.3 The Pipeline and Object-Based Output PowerShell's key differentiator
🔗 Pipeline: Objects, Not Text 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           BASH vs POWERSHELL PIPELINE                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   BASH (Text-based):                                            │
│   ┌─────────┐       ┌──────────┐       ┌─────────┐              │
│   │  ps aux │──────►│  grep    │──────►│  awk    │              │
│   └─────────┘ text  └──────────┘ text  └─────────┘              │
│                                                                  │
│   $ ps aux | grep python | awk '{print $2}'  # Parse text       │
│                                                                  │
│   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                                  │
│   POWERSHELL (Object-based):                                     │
│   ┌─────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐ │
│   │ Get-Process │──────►│ Where-Object │──────►│Select-Object │ │
│   └─────────────┘objects└──────────────┘objects└──────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│   Get-Process | Where Name -eq python | Select Id  # Properties │
│                                                                  │
│   ADVANTAGE: No text parsing! Access properties directly.       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Pipeline Operations
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PIPELINE FUNDAMENTALS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# See what type of object comes out of a cmdlet
Get-Process | Get-Member          # Shows all properties and methods

# $_ or $PSItem = current pipeline object
Get-Process | ForEach-Object { $_.Name }
Get-Process | ForEach { $PSItem.Name }   # Same thing

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FILTERING WITH WHERE-OBJECT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Full syntax
Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.CPU -gt 100 }

# Simplified syntax (v3+)
Get-Process | Where-Object CPU -gt 100

# Multiple conditions
Get-Service | Where-Object {
    $_.Status -eq "Running" -and $_.StartType -eq "Automatic"
}

# Alias: ? or Where
Get-Process | ? CPU -gt 100

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SELECTING WITH SELECT-OBJECT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Select specific properties
Get-Process | Select-Object Name, CPU, WorkingSet

# First/Last N items
Get-Process | Select-Object -First 5
Get-ChildItem | Select-Object -Last 3

# Create calculated properties
Get-Process | Select-Object Name, @{
    Name = "MemoryMB"
    Expression = { [math]::Round($_.WorkingSet / 1MB, 2) }
}

# Expand single property
Get-ChildItem | Select-Object -ExpandProperty FullName

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SORTING WITH SORT-OBJECT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending
Get-ChildItem | Sort-Object Length, Name

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GROUPING AND MEASURING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Group by property
Get-Service | Group-Object Status

# Measure (count, sum, average)
Get-Process | Measure-Object -Property WorkingSet -Sum -Average

# Count items
(Get-ChildItem -File).Count

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# REAL-WORLD PIPELINE EXAMPLE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Find large log files modified in last 7 days
Get-ChildItem -Path "C:\Logs" -Recurse -Filter "*.log" |
    Where-Object { $_.LastWriteTime -gt (Get-Date).AddDays(-7) } |
    Where-Object { $_.Length -gt 10MB } |
    Sort-Object Length -Descending |
    Select-Object Name, @{N="SizeMB"; E={[math]::Round($_.Length/1MB,2)}}, LastWriteTime |
    Format-Table -AutoSize
15.4 Variables and Data Types Working with PowerShell variables
📦 PowerShell Data Types 
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    POWERSHELL TYPES                               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Type          │ .NET Type      │ Example                         │
├───────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ [string]      │ System.String  │ "Hello World"                   │
│ [int]         │ System.Int32   │ 42                              │
│ [long]        │ System.Int64   │ 9999999999                      │
│ [double]      │ System.Double  │ 3.14159                         │
│ [decimal]     │ System.Decimal │ [decimal]3.14                   │
│ [bool]        │ System.Boolean │ $true, $false                   │
│ [datetime]    │ System.DateTime│ Get-Date                        │
│ [array]       │ System.Array   │ @(1, 2, 3)                      │
│ [hashtable]   │ System.Collections.Hashtable │ @{Key="Value"}   │
│ [psobject]    │ PSCustomObject │ [PSCustomObject]@{A=1}          │
└───────────────┴────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Variables and Collections
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VARIABLE BASICS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$name = "Alice"              # String
$count = 42                  # Integer
$pi = 3.14159               # Double
$enabled = $true            # Boolean
$nothing = $null            # Null

# Type casting
[int]$number = "42"         # String to int
[string]$text = 123         # Int to string

# Check type
$name.GetType().Name        # Returns: String
$name -is [string]          # Returns: True

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AUTOMATIC VARIABLES (Built-in)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$_   or $PSItem             # Current pipeline object
$?                          # Last command success (True/False)
$LASTEXITCODE              # Exit code of last native command
$Error                     # Array of recent errors
$PWD                       # Current directory
$HOME                      # User home directory
$env:USERNAME              # Environment variable
$PSVersionTable            # PowerShell version info
$args                      # Script arguments (in function/script)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ARRAYS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$colors = @("Red", "Green", "Blue")
$colors = "Red", "Green", "Blue"    # Same thing

$colors[0]                  # First element: Red
$colors[-1]                 # Last element: Blue
$colors[0..1]               # Slice: Red, Green
$colors += "Yellow"         # Add element
$colors.Count               # Length: 4

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# HASHTABLES (Dictionaries)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$server = @{
    Name      = "WebServer01"
    IP        = "192.168.1.100"
    OS        = "Windows Server 2022"
    Ports     = @(80, 443, 3389)
}

$server["Name"]            # Access by key
$server.IP                 # Dot notation
$server.Keys               # Get all keys
$server.Values             # Get all values
$server["Status"] = "Running"  # Add/update key

# Ordered hashtable (preserves key order)
$ordered = [ordered]@{
    First  = 1
    Second = 2
    Third  = 3
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CUSTOM OBJECTS (PSCustomObject)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$server = [PSCustomObject]@{
    Name   = "WebServer01"
    IP     = "192.168.1.100"
    Status = "Running"
}

# Access properties
$server.Name
$server.Status = "Stopped"  # Modify

# Collection of objects
$servers = @(
    [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = "Web01"; IP = "10.0.0.1" }
    [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = "Web02"; IP = "10.0.0.2" }
    [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = "DB01";  IP = "10.0.0.10" }
)

# Now you can use pipeline!
$servers | Where-Object Name -like "Web*"
15.6 PowerShell vs Bash: Key Differences Quick reference for shell scripters
⚖️ PowerShell vs Bash Comparison 
┌────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┐
│ Task               │ Bash                    │ PowerShell              │
├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
│ Variables          │ $var                    │ $var                    │
│ Assign variable    │ var="value" (no spaces!)│ $var = "value"          │
│ Environment vars   │ export VAR=value        │ $env:VAR = "value"      │
│ String concat      │ "$var1$var2"            │ "$var1$var2"            │
│ Command subst.     │ $(command) or `cmd`     │ $(command)              │
│ If statement       │ if [ cond ]; then fi    │ if (cond) { }           │
│ For loop           │ for i in list; do done  │ foreach ($i in $list) {}│
│ While loop         │ while cond; do done     │ while (cond) { }        │
│ Function           │ func() { ... }          │ function Func { ... }   │
│ Arguments          │ $1 $2 $@                │ $args[0] $args[1] $args │
│ Last exit code     │ $?                      │ $LASTEXITCODE           │
│ Pipe               │ |                       │ |                       │
│ Redirect output    │ > file                  │ > file or Out-File      │
│ Redirect stderr    │ 2> file                 │ 2> file                 │
│ Both to file       │ &> file                 │ *> file                 │
│ Null redirect      │ > /dev/null             │ > $null or Out-Null     │
│ AND operator       │ &&                       │ -and (or ; or && in 7+) │
│ OR operator        │ ||                      │ -or (or || in 7+)       │
│ String compare     │ [ "$a" = "$b" ]         │ $a -eq $b               │
│ Numeric compare    │ [ $a -eq $b ]           │ $a -eq $b               │
│ File exists        │ [ -f file ]             │ Test-Path file          │
│ Array              │ arr=(a b c)             │ $arr = @("a","b","c")   │
│ Array access       │ ${arr[0]}               │ $arr[0]                 │
│ Get help           │ man command             │ Get-Help Cmdlet         │
│ List files         │ ls -la                  │ Get-ChildItem           │
│ Current directory  │ pwd                     │ Get-Location            │
│ Read file          │ cat file                │ Get-Content file        │
│ Write file         │ echo "x" > file         │ Set-Content file "x"    │
└────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘

Chapter 16: PowerShell Scripting Essentials

Writing professional PowerShell scripts

Chapter Topics

16.1 Writing PowerShell Scripts (.ps1)
16.2 Execution Policies
16.3 Control Flow (if, switch, loops)
16.4 Functions and Modules
16.5 Error Handling (try/catch/finally)
16.6 Working with Objects and Properties
16.7 Remote Execution and Sessions
16.8 PowerShell Desired State Configuration (DSC)
16.1 Writing PowerShell Scripts (.ps1) Professional script structure and best practices
📝 PowerShell Script Structure 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              RECOMMENDED .PS1 SCRIPT STRUCTURE                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 1. Comment-Based Help Block               │  <-- Required   │
│   │    .SYNOPSIS, .DESCRIPTION, .EXAMPLE      │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    ↓                                            │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 2. #Requires Statement (optional)         │                 │
│   │    #Requires -Version 7.0                 │                 │
│   │    #Requires -Modules Az                  │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    ↓                                            │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 3. Parameter Block [CmdletBinding()]      │  <-- Important  │
│   │    param() with validation                │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    ↓                                            │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 4. Functions (defined before use)         │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    ↓                                            │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 5. Main Script Logic                      │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    ↓                                            │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐                 │
│   │ 6. Return/Output                          │                 │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Complete Script Template
<#
.SYNOPSIS
    Brief description of what the script does.

.DESCRIPTION
    Detailed description of the script's purpose, behavior, and requirements.

.PARAMETER ServerName
    The name of the server to connect to.

.PARAMETER Environment
    The target environment (Dev, Test, Prod).

.EXAMPLE
    .\Deploy-Application.ps1 -ServerName "WebServer01" -Environment "Prod"
    Deploys to WebServer01 in production.

.EXAMPLE
    Get-Content servers.txt | .\Deploy-Application.ps1 -Environment "Dev"
    Deploys to all servers listed in servers.txt.

.NOTES
    Author: Your Name
    Date:   2025-06-21
    Version: 1.0
#>

#Requires -Version 7.0
#Requires -Modules Az.Accounts, Az.Resources

[CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess)]
param(
    [Parameter(Mandatory, ValueFromPipeline, Position = 0)]
    [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
    [string]$ServerName,

    [Parameter(Mandatory)]
    [ValidateSet("Dev", "Test", "Prod")]
    [string]$Environment,

    [Parameter()]
    [ValidateRange(1, 3600)]
    [int]$TimeoutSeconds = 300,

    [Parameter()]
    [switch]$Force
)

begin {
    # Runs once before processing pipeline input
    Write-Verbose "Starting deployment to $Environment environment"
    $startTime = Get-Date
}

process {
    # Runs for each pipeline item (or once if no pipeline)
    if ($PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess($ServerName, "Deploy Application")) {
        Write-Host "Deploying to $ServerName..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
        # ... deployment logic here ...
    }
}

end {
    # Runs once after processing all pipeline input
    $duration = (Get-Date) - $startTime
    Write-Verbose "Completed in $($duration.TotalSeconds) seconds"
}
16.2 Execution Policies Understanding script execution security
🔒 Execution Policy Levels 
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Policy          │ Description                                    │
├─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Restricted      │ No scripts allowed (Windows client default)   │
│ AllSigned       │ All scripts must be signed by trusted CA      │
│ RemoteSigned    │ Downloaded scripts must be signed (Server)    │
│ Unrestricted    │ All scripts allowed, warns on downloaded      │
│ Bypass          │ No restrictions, no warnings (CI/CD use)      │
│ Undefined       │ No policy set at this scope                   │
└─────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────┘

SCOPE PRECEDENCE (highest to lowest):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MachinePolicy  >  UserPolicy  >  Process  >  CurrentUser  >  LocalMachine │
│   (GPO)            (GPO)       (session)    (user reg)     (machine reg)   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Managing Execution Policy
# Check current policy
Get-ExecutionPolicy
Get-ExecutionPolicy -List    # Show all scopes

# Set for current user (persists)
Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

# Set for this session only (non-persistent)
Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process

# Run single script bypassing policy
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ".\script.ps1"

# Unblock downloaded script (removes Zone.Identifier)
Unblock-File -Path ".\downloaded-script.ps1"

# Check if file is blocked
Get-Item ".\script.ps1" -Stream Zone.Identifier -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
16.3 Control Flow (if, switch, loops) Conditional logic and iteration
▶️ Control Flow Patterns
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# IF / ELSEIF / ELSE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$status = "Running"

if ($status -eq "Running") {
    Write-Host "Service is running" -ForegroundColor Green
}
elseif ($status -eq "Stopped") {
    Write-Host "Service is stopped" -ForegroundColor Red
}
else {
    Write-Host "Unknown status: $status" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

# Comparison operators: -eq -ne -gt -lt -ge -le -like -match -in -contains

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SWITCH STATEMENT
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

$day = (Get-Date).DayOfWeek

switch ($day) {
    "Monday"    { "Start of week" }
    "Friday"    { "TGIF!" }
    "Saturday"  { "Weekend!" }
    "Sunday"    { "Weekend!" }
    default     { "Midweek" }
}

# Switch with pattern matching
switch -Regex ($filename) {
    '\.log$'  { "Log file" }
    '\.txt$'  { "Text file" }
    '\.json$' { "JSON file" }
    default   { "Unknown type" }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LOOPS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ForEach loop
$servers = @("Web01", "Web02", "DB01")
foreach ($server in $servers) {
    Write-Host "Processing: $server"
}

# ForEach-Object (pipeline version)
$servers | ForEach-Object {
    Write-Host "Processing: $_"
}

# For loop (traditional)
for ($i = 0; $i -lt 5; $i++) {
    Write-Host "Iteration $i"
}

# While loop
$count = 0
while ($count -lt 5) {
    Write-Host "Count: $count"
    $count++
}

# Do-While (executes at least once)
do {
    $input = Read-Host "Enter 'quit' to exit"
} while ($input -ne "quit")

# Do-Until
do {
    $response = Invoke-RestMethod $url
    Start-Sleep -Seconds 5
} until ($response.status -eq "Complete")

# Loop control
break     # Exit loop entirely
continue  # Skip to next iteration
16.4 Functions and Modules Reusable code organization
▶️ Advanced Functions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ADVANCED FUNCTION (Cmdlet-style)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

function Get-ServerHealth {
    <#
    .SYNOPSIS
        Gets health status of a server.
    .PARAMETER ComputerName
        The server to check.
    .OUTPUTS
        PSCustomObject with health data.
    #>
    [CmdletBinding()]
    [OutputType([PSCustomObject])]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory, ValueFromPipeline)]
        [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
        [Alias("Server", "Host")]
        [string]$ComputerName,

        [Parameter()]
        [ValidateSet("Quick", "Full")]
        [string]$Mode = "Quick",

        [Parameter()]
        [PSCredential]$Credential
    )

    begin {
        Write-Verbose "Starting health check (Mode: $Mode)"
    }

    process {
        Write-Verbose "Checking: $ComputerName"

        # Ping test
        $ping = Test-Connection -ComputerName $ComputerName -Count 1 -Quiet

        [PSCustomObject]@{
            ComputerName = $ComputerName
            Online       = $ping
            CheckTime    = Get-Date
            Mode         = $Mode
        }
    }

    end {
        Write-Verbose "Health check complete"
    }
}

# Usage
Get-ServerHealth -ComputerName "Server01" -Verbose
"Web01", "Web02" | Get-ServerHealth -Mode Full

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CREATING A MODULE (.psm1)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# File: C:\Users\You\Documents\PowerShell\Modules\ServerTools\ServerTools.psm1

# --- ServerTools.psm1 ---
function Get-ServerHealth { ... }
function Restart-ServerService { ... }
function Get-ServerLogs { ... }

# Export specific functions
Export-ModuleMember -Function Get-ServerHealth, Restart-ServerService

# --- End ServerTools.psm1 ---

# Using the module
Import-Module ServerTools
Get-ServerHealth -ComputerName "Web01"

# Auto-load from standard paths:
# - $env:PSModulePath locations
# - $HOME\Documents\PowerShell\Modules\
16.5 Error Handling (try/catch/finally) Robust error management
⚠️ PowerShell Error Types 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              ERROR TYPES IN POWERSHELL                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   TERMINATING ERRORS                                            │
│   • Stop script execution                                       │
│   • Can be caught with try/catch                                │
│   • Examples: throw, cmdlets with -ErrorAction Stop             │
│                                                                  │
│   NON-TERMINATING ERRORS                                        │
│   • Reported but execution continues                            │
│   • Default for most cmdlet errors                              │
│   • Use -ErrorAction Stop to make terminating                   │
│   • Stored in $Error automatic variable                         │
│                                                                  │
│   -ErrorAction Values: Stop | Continue | SilentlyContinue |     │
│                        Ignore | Inquire | Suspend               │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Error Handling Patterns
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BASIC TRY/CATCH/FINALLY
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

try {
    # Force terminating error with -ErrorAction Stop
    Get-Content "nonexistent.txt" -ErrorAction Stop
}
catch {
    Write-Error "Failed to read file: $_"
    # $_ contains the error object
}
finally {
    # Always runs (cleanup code)
    Write-Verbose "Cleanup complete"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CATCH SPECIFIC EXCEPTIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

try {
    $result = 10 / 0
}
catch [System.DivideByZeroException] {
    Write-Error "Cannot divide by zero"
}
catch [System.IO.FileNotFoundException] {
    Write-Error "File not found"
}
catch {
    # Catch-all for other errors
    Write-Error "Unexpected error: $($_.Exception.Message)"
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# ERROR DETAILS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

try {
    Get-Content "missing.txt" -ErrorAction Stop
}
catch {
    $_.Exception.Message          # Error message
    $_.Exception.GetType().Name   # Exception type
    $_.InvocationInfo.ScriptName  # Script that caused error
    $_.InvocationInfo.ScriptLineNumber  # Line number
    $_.ScriptStackTrace           # Full stack trace
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PRODUCTION ERROR HANDLING PATTERN
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

function Invoke-SafeOperation {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory)]
        [ScriptBlock]$ScriptBlock,

        [Parameter()]
        [int]$MaxRetries = 3,

        [Parameter()]
        [int]$RetryDelaySeconds = 5
    )

    $attempt = 0
    while ($attempt -lt $MaxRetries) {
        $attempt++
        try {
            return & $ScriptBlock
        }
        catch {
            Write-Warning "Attempt $attempt failed: $_"
            if ($attempt -lt $MaxRetries) {
                Write-Verbose "Retrying in $RetryDelaySeconds seconds..."
                Start-Sleep -Seconds $RetryDelaySeconds
            }
            else {
                throw "Operation failed after $MaxRetries attempts: $_"
            }
        }
    }
}

# Usage
Invoke-SafeOperation -MaxRetries 3 -ScriptBlock {
    Invoke-RestMethod "https://api.example.com/health"
}

Chapter 17: PowerShell for Cloud Automation

Managing cloud resources with PowerShell

Chapter Topics

17.1 Azure PowerShell Module (Az)
17.2 AWS Tools for PowerShell
17.3 Managing Azure Resources
17.4 Active Directory Automation
17.5 Windows Server Automation
17.6 Hyper-V Automation
17.7 Exchange and Office 365 Automation
17.1 Azure PowerShell Module (Az) Managing Azure with PowerShell
☁️ Azure PowerShell Module Structure 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AZURE POWERSHELL MODULES                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   Az (Main Module)                                              │
│   ├── Az.Accounts      ─ Authentication & subscription         │
│   ├── Az.Resources     ─ Resource groups, deployments           │
│   ├── Az.Compute       ─ VMs, VMSS, disks                       │
│   ├── Az.Network       ─ VNets, NICs, load balancers            │
│   ├── Az.Storage       ─ Storage accounts, blobs                │
│   ├── Az.KeyVault      ─ Secrets, keys, certificates            │
│   ├── Az.ContainerRegistry ─ ACR management                     │
│   ├── Az.Aks           ─ AKS clusters                           │
│   ├── Az.Functions     ─ Azure Functions                        │
│   ├── Az.Sql           ─ Azure SQL databases                    │
│   └── 60+ more modules...                                       │
│                                                                  │
│   Install: Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser           │
│   Update:  Update-Module -Name Az                               │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Azure PowerShell Essentials
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALLATION AND AUTHENTICATION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Install Azure PowerShell
Install-Module -Name Az -Repository PSGallery -Force -Scope CurrentUser

# Interactive login
Connect-AzAccount

# Service principal login (CI/CD)
$credential = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential(
    $env:AZURE_CLIENT_ID,
    (ConvertTo-SecureString $env:AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET -AsPlainText -Force)
)
Connect-AzAccount -ServicePrincipal -Credential $credential -Tenant $env:AZURE_TENANT_ID

# Managed Identity login (Azure VMs, Functions)
Connect-AzAccount -Identity

# Select subscription
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
Get-AzContext  # View current context

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON AZURE OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Resource Groups
Get-AzResourceGroup
New-AzResourceGroup -Name "rg-myapp-prod" -Location "eastus"
Remove-AzResourceGroup -Name "rg-myapp-dev" -Force

# List all resources in subscription
Get-AzResource | Format-Table Name, ResourceType, ResourceGroupName

# Get resources by type
Get-AzResource -ResourceType "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines"

# Tags
Get-AzResource -Tag @{Environment = "Production"}
Set-AzResource -ResourceId $vm.Id -Tag @{Environment = "Prod"; Team = "DevOps"}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VIRTUAL MACHINES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# List VMs
Get-AzVM -Status | Select-Object Name, ResourceGroupName, PowerState

# Start/Stop VMs
Start-AzVM -ResourceGroupName "rg-prod" -Name "vm-web01"
Stop-AzVM -ResourceGroupName "rg-prod" -Name "vm-web01" -Force

# Create VM (simplified)
$vmConfig = @{
    ResourceGroupName   = "rg-prod"
    Name                = "vm-web01"
    Location            = "eastus"
    VirtualNetworkName  = "vnet-prod"
    SubnetName          = "snet-web"
    SecurityGroupName   = "nsg-web"
    PublicIpAddressName = "pip-web01"
    Credential          = Get-Credential
}
New-AzVm @vmConfig -Image "Win2022Datacenter"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STORAGE OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Get storage account context
$storage = Get-AzStorageAccount -ResourceGroupName "rg-prod" -Name "stproddata"
$ctx = $storage.Context

# List containers
Get-AzStorageContainer -Context $ctx

# Upload blob
Set-AzStorageBlobContent -File "local.txt" -Container "uploads" -Blob "remote.txt" -Context $ctx

# Download blob
Get-AzStorageBlobContent -Container "uploads" -Blob "remote.txt" -Destination "downloaded.txt" -Context $ctx
17.2 AWS Tools for PowerShell Managing AWS with PowerShell
🔶 AWS vs Azure PowerShell Comparison 
┌────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
│ Operation              │ Azure PowerShell     │ AWS PowerShell       │
├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Install                │ Install-Module Az    │ Install-Module AWS.. │
│ Login (interactive)    │ Connect-AzAccount    │ Set-AWSCredential    │
│ List VMs/Instances     │ Get-AzVM             │ Get-EC2Instance      │
│ Start VM               │ Start-AzVM           │ Start-EC2Instance    │
│ Stop VM                │ Stop-AzVM            │ Stop-EC2Instance     │
│ List storage           │ Get-AzStorageAccount │ Get-S3Bucket         │
│ List resource groups   │ Get-AzResourceGroup  │ (N/A - use tags)     │
│ Set region/context     │ Set-AzContext        │ Set-DefaultAWSRegion │
└────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
▶️ AWS PowerShell Essentials
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALLATION AND AUTHENTICATION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Install AWS Tools (modular - install what you need)
Install-Module -Name AWS.Tools.Installer -Force
Install-AWSToolsModule AWS.Tools.EC2, AWS.Tools.S3, AWS.Tools.IAM

# Or install all at once (larger)
Install-Module -Name AWSPowerShell.NetCore -Force

# Configure credentials
Set-AWSCredential -AccessKey "AKIA..." -SecretKey "..." -StoreAs "default"

# Use profile
Set-AWSCredential -ProfileName "production"

# Set default region
Set-DefaultAWSRegion -Region "us-east-1"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EC2 OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# List instances
Get-EC2Instance | ForEach-Object { $_.Instances } |
    Select-Object InstanceId, InstanceType, @{N="Name"; E={($_.Tags | Where Key -eq "Name").Value}}

# Start/Stop instances
Start-EC2Instance -InstanceId "i-0123456789"
Stop-EC2Instance -InstanceId "i-0123456789" -Force

# Get instance status
Get-EC2InstanceStatus -InstanceId "i-0123456789"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# S3 OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# List buckets
Get-S3Bucket

# List objects in bucket
Get-S3Object -BucketName "my-bucket" -KeyPrefix "logs/"

# Upload file
Write-S3Object -BucketName "my-bucket" -Key "uploads/file.txt" -File "local.txt"

# Download file
Read-S3Object -BucketName "my-bucket" -Key "uploads/file.txt" -File "downloaded.txt"

🏗️ Part 5: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

Automate infrastructure provisioning with Terraform, CloudFormation, and Ansible.

Chapter 18: IaC Fundamentals

Core concepts of Infrastructure as Code

Chapter Topics

18.1 What is Infrastructure as Code?
18.2 Declarative vs Imperative Approaches
18.3 IaC Tools Comparison (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi, CDK)
18.4 State Management Concepts
18.5 Idempotency and Drift
18.6 IaC Best Practices
18.7 Testing Infrastructure Code
18.8 IaC Security Considerations
18.1 What is Infrastructure as Code? Understanding the IaC paradigm
🏗️ Infrastructure as Code Overview 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TRADITIONAL vs IaC APPROACH                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   TRADITIONAL (Manual)           IaC (Automated)                │
│   ┌─────────────────┐            ┌─────────────────┐            │
│   │  Click through  │            │  main.tf        │            │
│   │  cloud console  │            │  ───────────    │            │
│   │  ┌───┐ ┌───┐   │            │  resource "..." │            │
│   │  │ ▢ │ │ ▢ │   │            │    name = "..."│             │
│   │  └───┘ └───┘   │            │  }              │            │
│   └─────────────────┘            └─────────────────┘            │
│           │                              │                      │
│           ▼                              ▼                      │
│   - Not repeatable              - Version controlled           │
│   - Error-prone                 - Peer reviewable              │
│   - No audit trail              - Consistent                   │
│   - Slow at scale               - Fast & repeatable            │
│   - Knowledge in heads          - Self-documenting             │
│                                                                  │
│   KEY BENEFITS OF IaC:                                          │
│   ✓ Reproducibility  - Deploy same infra anywhere              │
│   ✓ Version Control  - Track changes, rollback                 │
│   ✓ Automation       - CI/CD integration                       │
│   ✓ Documentation    - Code IS the documentation               │
│   ✓ Collaboration    - Team reviews, shared ownership          │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
18.2 Declarative vs Imperative Approaches Two paradigms for defining infrastructure
⚖️ Declarative vs Imperative 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DECLARATIVE vs IMPERATIVE                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   DECLARATIVE (WHAT)                IMPERATIVE (HOW)            │
│   "I want 3 servers"                "Create server 1"           │
│                                     "Create server 2"           │
│                                     "Create server 3"           │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────┐         │
│   │ DESIRED STATE   │               │ STEP-BY-STEP    │         │
│   │ ─────────────── │               │ INSTRUCTIONS    │         │
│   │ servers: 3      │               │ ─────────────── │         │
│   │ size: large     │               │ 1. Check if...  │         │
│   │ region: us-east │               │ 2. If not, do...│         │
│   └─────────────────┘               │ 3. Then do...   │         │
│           │                         │ 4. Finally...   │         │
│           ▼                         └─────────────────┘         │
│   Tool figures out HOW                      │                   │
│                                             ▼                   │
│   TOOLS:                            TOOLS:                      │
│   • Terraform                       • Bash/Python scripts       │
│   • CloudFormation                  • Ansible (can be both)     │
│   • Pulumi                          • AWS CLI scripts           │
│   • Kubernetes YAML                 • Chef/Puppet (procedural)  │
│                                                                  │
│   ADVANTAGES:                       ADVANTAGES:                  │
│   ✓ Simpler to understand          ✓ Full control               │
│   ✓ Idempotent by design           ✓ Complex logic possible     │
│   ✓ Tool handles dependencies      ✓ Easier for migrations      │
│   ✓ Drift detection built-in                                    │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
18.3 IaC Tools Comparison Choosing the right tool
🔧 IaC Tools Matrix 
┌───────────────────┬──────────┬───────────┬────────────┬────────────┐
│ Tool              │ Lang     │ State     │ Cloud      │ Best For   │
├───────────────────┼──────────┼───────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
│ Terraform         │ HCL      │ External  │ Multi      │ Multi-cloud│
│ CloudFormation    │ YAML/JSON│ AWS-mgd   │ AWS only   │ AWS-native │
│ Pulumi            │ TS/Py/Go │ Cloud/Self│ Multi      │ Dev teams  │
│ CDK (AWS)         │ TS/Py/Ja │ AWS-mgd   │ AWS only   │ AWS devs   │
│ Bicep             │ Bicep    │ Azure-mgd │ Azure only │ Azure-native│
│ ARM Templates     │ JSON     │ Azure-mgd │ Azure only │ Azure legacy│
│ Ansible           │ YAML     │ Stateless │ Multi      │ Config mgmt│
│ Crossplane        │ YAML     │ K8s CRD   │ Multi      │ K8s-native │
└───────────────────┴──────────┴───────────┴────────────┴────────────┘

DECISION GUIDE:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ "Which IaC tool should I use?"                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│ Multi-cloud / Cloud agnostic? ──────────► Terraform            │
│                                                                  │
│ AWS only, prefer native? ───────────────► CloudFormation/CDK    │
│                                                                  │
│ Azure only, prefer native? ─────────────► Bicep                 │
│                                                                  │
│ Team knows Python/TypeScript? ──────────► Pulumi or CDK         │
│                                                                  │
│ Configuration management focus? ────────► Ansible               │
│                                                                  │
│ Kubernetes-native GitOps? ──────────────► Crossplane            │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
18.4 State Management Concepts Understanding IaC state
📦 State: The Heart of IaC 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              IaC STATE EXPLAINED                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   STATE = Record of what infrastructure IaC tool has created    │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌────────────────┐   ┌────────────────┐   ┌────────────────┐  │
│   │   YOUR CODE    │   │     STATE      │   │  REAL CLOUD    │  │
│   │   (Desired)    │   │   (Known)      │   │  (Actual)      │  │
│   ├────────────────┤   ├────────────────┤   ├────────────────┤  │
│   │ VM: web01     │   │ VM: web01     │   │ VM: web01     │   │
│   │   size: large  │   │   id: i-123    │   │   size: large  │  │
│   │                │   │   size: large  │   │                │  │
│   └────────────────┘   └────────────────┘   └────────────────┘  │
│                                                                  │
│   WHY STATE MATTERS:                                            │
│   • Maps code resources to real cloud resources                 │
│   • Tracks metadata (IDs, ARNs) that code doesn't have          │
│   • Enables incremental updates (only change what's needed)     │
│   • Detects drift (actual != state)                             │
│                                                                  │
│   STATE STORAGE OPTIONS:                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ Local File      │ terraform.tfstate (don't use in team) │   │
│   │ S3 + DynamoDB   │ AWS backend with locking              │   │
│   │ Azure Blob      │ Azure backend with locking            │   │
│   │ GCS             │ Google Cloud backend                  │   │
│   │ Terraform Cloud │ HashiCorp hosted backend              │   │
│   │ Spacelift/env0  │ Third-party IaC platforms             │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Chapter 19: Terraform Automation

Automating Terraform workflows and operations

Chapter Topics

19.1 Terraform Workflow (init, plan, apply, destroy)
19.2 Scripting Terraform Operations
19.3 Automated Terraform with CI/CD
19.4 Terraform Modules Automation
19.5 State Management Scripts
19.6 Terraform Import Automation
19.7 Cost Estimation Automation
19.8 Policy as Code (Sentinel, OPA)
19.9 Terragrunt for DRY Terraform
19.1 Terraform Workflow Core Terraform commands and lifecycle
🔄 Terraform Workflow Diagram 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TERRAFORM CORE WORKFLOW                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌──────────┐  │
│   │  init   │────►│  plan   │────►│  apply  │────►│ destroy  │  │
│   └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └──────────┘  │
│        │               │               │                        │
│        ▼               ▼               ▼                        │
│   Download        Compare          Execute          Delete      │
│   providers       desired vs       changes          resources   │
│   Initialize      actual                                        │
│   backend         state                                         │
│                                                                  │
│   ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════   │
│                                                                  │
│   SECONDARY COMMANDS:                                           │
│   ┌────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ validate   │ Check syntax without accessing cloud       │   │
│   │ fmt        │ Format code to canonical style             │   │
│   │ show       │ Display current state or plan             │   │
│   │ output     │ Extract output values                      │   │
│   │ import     │ Import existing resources into state       │   │
│   │ state      │ Direct state manipulation                  │   │
│   │ refresh    │ Update state from real infrastructure      │   │
│   │ taint      │ Mark resource for recreation (deprecated)  │   │
│   └────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ Terraform Commands Reference
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INITIALIZATION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Initialize working directory (download providers, setup backend)
terraform init

# Reinitialize (e.g., after backend change)
terraform init -reconfigure

# Upgrade providers to latest versions
terraform init -upgrade

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PLANNING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Generate execution plan
terraform plan

# Save plan to file (recommended for CI/CD)
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan with variable file
terraform plan -var-file="prod.tfvars"

# Plan for specific target
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web

# Plan destroy (preview what will be deleted)
terraform plan -destroy

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# APPLYING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Apply changes (with confirmation prompt)
terraform apply

# Apply saved plan (no prompt)
terraform apply tfplan

# Auto-approve (CI/CD usage)
terraform apply -auto-approve

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DESTROYING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Destroy all resources
terraform destroy

# Destroy with auto-approve
terraform destroy -auto-approve

# Destroy specific resource
terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# UTILITY COMMANDS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Format code
terraform fmt -recursive

# Validate configuration
terraform validate

# Show current state
terraform show

# Show output values
terraform output
terraform output -json

# List resources in state
terraform state list

# Show specific resource
terraform state show aws_instance.web
19.3 Automated Terraform with CI/CD GitHub Actions and GitLab CI examples
▶️ GitHub Actions Terraform Workflow
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['terraform/**']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['terraform/**']

env:
  TF_VERSION: '1.7.0'
  WORKING_DIR: './terraform'

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ${{ env.WORKING_DIR }}
    
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
      
      - name: Terraform Format Check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -backend-config="bucket=${{ secrets.TF_STATE_BUCKET }}"
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
      
      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate
      
      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -out=tfplan
        continue-on-error: true
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
      
      - name: Post Plan to PR
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const plan = `${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout }}`;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `#### Terraform Plan 📖\n\`\`\`hcl\n${plan.slice(0, 65000)}\n\`\`\``
            });
      
      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
▶️ Terraform Wrapper Script
#!/bin/bash
# terraform-wrapper.sh - Safe Terraform execution wrapper

set -euo pipefail

ENVIRONMENT="${1:-dev}"
ACTION="${2:-plan}"
TF_DIR="./environments/${ENVIRONMENT}"

# Validation
if [[ ! -d "$TF_DIR" ]]; then
    echo "❌ Environment '$ENVIRONMENT' not found in $TF_DIR"
    exit 1
fi

cd "$TF_DIR"

echo "🔧 Running Terraform $ACTION for $ENVIRONMENT"

# Initialize
terraform init -input=false

# Format check
terraform fmt -check -recursive || {
    echo "❌ Terraform formatting issues found. Run 'terraform fmt -recursive'"
    exit 1
}

# Validate
terraform validate

case "$ACTION" in
    plan)
        terraform plan -out=tfplan
        ;;
    apply)
        if [[ "$ENVIRONMENT" == "prod" ]]; then
            echo "⚠️  Production apply requires explicit confirmation"
            terraform apply tfplan
        else
            terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
        fi
        ;;
    destroy)
        if [[ "$ENVIRONMENT" == "prod" ]]; then
            echo "🛑 Production destroy blocked by policy"
            exit 1
        fi
        terraform destroy -auto-approve
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Unknown action: $ACTION"
        echo "Usage: $0 <environment> <plan|apply|destroy>"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

echo "✅ Terraform $ACTION completed successfully"

20.1 Stack Management Scripts Automating CloudFormation stacks
🔄 CloudFormation Stack Lifecycle 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CLOUDFORMATION WORKFLOW                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   ┌──────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌───────────┐ │
│   │ Create   │  │ Change Set  │  │   Update    │  │  Delete   │ │
│   │ Stack    │──│  (Review)   │──│   Stack     │──│  Stack    │ │
│   └──────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └───────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│   CREATE_IN_PROGRESS → CREATE_COMPLETE                          │
│   UPDATE_IN_PROGRESS → UPDATE_COMPLETE                          │
│   DELETE_IN_PROGRESS → DELETE_COMPLETE                          │
│                                                                  │
│   ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS → ROLLBACK_COMPLETE (on failure)        │
│                                                                  │
│   AWS CLI COMMANDS:                                             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ aws cloudformation create-stack                         │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation update-stack                         │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation delete-stack                         │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation describe-stacks                      │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation describe-stack-events                │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation create-change-set                    │   │
│   │ aws cloudformation execute-change-set                   │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
▶️ CloudFormation Stack Automation Script
#!/bin/bash
# cfn-deploy.sh - CloudFormation deployment wrapper

set -euo pipefail

STACK_NAME="${1:-}"
TEMPLATE_FILE="${2:-template.yaml}"
PARAMETERS_FILE="${3:-parameters.json}"
REGION="${AWS_REGION:-us-east-1}"

usage() {
    echo "Usage: $0 <stack-name> [template-file] [parameters-file]"
    exit 1
}

[[ -z "$STACK_NAME" ]] && usage

# Check if stack exists
stack_exists() {
    aws cloudformation describe-stacks \
        --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
        --region "$REGION" >/dev/null 2>&1
}

# Wait for stack operation to complete
wait_for_stack() {
    local operation=$1
    echo "⏳ Waiting for $operation..."
    aws cloudformation wait "stack-${operation}-complete" \
        --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
        --region "$REGION"
    echo "✅ Stack $operation complete"
}

# View recent stack events
show_events() {
    aws cloudformation describe-stack-events \
        --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
        --region "$REGION" \
        --query 'StackEvents[0:10].[Timestamp,ResourceStatus,LogicalResourceId,ResourceStatusReason]' \
        --output table
}

# Deploy stack
deploy() {
    local params=""
    [[ -f "$PARAMETERS_FILE" ]] && params="--parameters file://$PARAMETERS_FILE"
    
    if stack_exists; then
        echo "📦 Updating existing stack: $STACK_NAME"
        aws cloudformation update-stack \
            --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
            --template-body "file://$TEMPLATE_FILE" \
            $params \
            --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM \
            --region "$REGION" || {
                [[ $? -eq 255 ]] && echo "No changes to deploy" && exit 0
            }
        wait_for_stack "update"
    else
        echo "🚀 Creating new stack: $STACK_NAME"
        aws cloudformation create-stack \
            --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
            --template-body "file://$TEMPLATE_FILE" \
            $params \
            --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM \
            --region "$REGION"
        wait_for_stack "create"
    fi
}

# Show outputs
show_outputs() {
    echo "📤 Stack Outputs:"
    aws cloudformation describe-stacks \
        --stack-name "$STACK_NAME" \
        --region "$REGION" \
        --query 'Stacks[0].Outputs' \
        --output table
}

deploy
show_outputs

21.1 Ansible Architecture and Concepts Understanding Ansible's components
📐 Ansible Architecture 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              ANSIBLE ARCHITECTURE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              CONTROL NODE (Your Machine)                 │   │
│   │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  │   │
│   │  │ Playbooks  │  │ Inventory  │  │ ansible.cfg       │  │   │
│   │  │ (.yaml)    │  │ (hosts)    │  │ (config)          │  │   │
│   │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  │   │
│   │                       │                                  │   │
│   │                       ▼                                  │   │
│   │              ┌────────────────┐                         │   │
│   │              │ Ansible Engine │                         │   │
│   │              │  (ansible-*)   │                         │   │
│   │              └────────────────┘                         │   │
│   └──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                          │ SSH (agentless)                      │
│                          ▼                                      │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│   │                    MANAGED NODES                          │  │
│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐     │  │
│   │  │ web01   │  │ web02   │  │  db01   │  │ cache01 │     │  │
│   │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘     │  │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                  │
│   KEY CONCEPTS:                                                 │
│   • Agentless - Only needs SSH and Python on targets            │
│   • Push-based - Control node pushes changes (vs pull)          │
│   • Idempotent - Running twice produces same result             │
│   • Declarative - Describe desired state, not steps             │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
21.2 Inventory Management Defining hosts and groups
▶️ Ansible Inventory Formats
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# INI FORMAT (inventory.ini)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[webservers]
web01.example.com ansible_user=deploy
web02.example.com ansible_user=deploy

[dbservers]
db01.example.com ansible_user=admin ansible_port=2222

[production:children]
webservers
dbservers

[production:vars]
ansible_python_interpreter=/usr/bin/python3
env=production

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# YAML FORMAT (inventory.yaml)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

all:
  children:
    webservers:
      hosts:
        web01.example.com:
          ansible_user: deploy
        web02.example.com:
          ansible_user: deploy
    
    dbservers:
      hosts:
        db01.example.com:
          ansible_user: admin
          ansible_port: 2222
    
    production:
      children:
        webservers:
        dbservers:
      vars:
        ansible_python_interpreter: /usr/bin/python3
        env: production
21.3 Playbooks and Roles Organizing automation code
▶️ Ansible Playbook Examples
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BASIC PLAYBOOK (deploy-app.yaml)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

---
- name: Deploy anihpj Application
  hosts: webservers
  become: yes
  vars:
    app_name: anihpj
    app_version: "{{ version | default('latest') }}"
    app_dir: /opt/{{ app_name }}
  
  tasks:
    - name: Ensure application directory exists
      file:
        path: "{{ app_dir }}"
        state: directory
        owner: www-data
        mode: '0755'
    
    - name: Download application package
      get_url:
        url: "https://releases.example.com/{{ app_name }}/{{ app_version }}.tar.gz"
        dest: "/tmp/{{ app_name }}.tar.gz"
        checksum: "sha256:{{ package_checksum }}"
      register: download_result
    
    - name: Extract application
      unarchive:
        src: "/tmp/{{ app_name }}.tar.gz"
        dest: "{{ app_dir }}"
        remote_src: yes
      when: download_result.changed
    
    - name: Configure application
      template:
        src: config.ini.j2
        dest: "{{ app_dir }}/config.ini"
        owner: www-data
        mode: '0640'
      notify: Restart application
    
    - name: Ensure application service is running
      systemd:
        name: "{{ app_name }}"
        state: started
        enabled: yes
  
  handlers:
    - name: Restart application
      systemd:
        name: "{{ app_name }}"
        state: restarted

# Usage: ansible-playbook deploy-app.yaml -e "version=2.1.0"
📁 Ansible Role Directory Structure 
roles/
└── webserver/
    ├── defaults/
    │   └── main.yaml       # Default variables (lowest priority)
    ├── files/
    │   └── nginx.conf      # Static files to copy
    ├── handlers/
    │   └── main.yaml       # Handler definitions
    ├── meta/
    │   └── main.yaml       # Role dependencies
    ├── tasks/
    │   └── main.yaml       # Main task list
    ├── templates/
    │   └── vhost.conf.j2   # Jinja2 templates
    ├── vars/
    │   └── main.yaml       # Role variables (high priority)
    └── README.md

# Using roles in playbook:
---
- hosts: webservers
  roles:
    - webserver
    - { role: database, when: install_db }
    - { role: monitoring, tags: ['monitoring'] }

22.1 What is Configuration Management? Core concepts and benefits
⚙️ Configuration Management Overview 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   DEFINITION:                                                   │
│   Automating the process of maintaining systems in a            │
│   desired, consistent state                                     │
│                                                                  │
│   WITHOUT CM:                       WITH CM:                    │
│   ┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────┐         │
│   │ Server 1 ✓     │               │ Server 1 ✓     │         │
│   │ - nginx 1.18   │               │ - nginx 1.24   │         │
│   │ - Python 3.8   │               │ - Python 3.11  │         │
│   │ - /etc configs? │               │ - /etc matches │         │
│   └─────────────────┘               └─────────────────┘         │
│   ┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────┐         │
│   │ Server 2 ??    │               │ Server 2 ✓     │         │
│   │ - nginx 1.20   │               │ - nginx 1.24   │         │
│   │ - Python 3.10  │   ────►       │ - Python 3.11  │         │
│   │ - manual edits │               │ - /etc matches │         │
│   └─────────────────┘               └─────────────────┘         │
│   ┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────┐         │
│   │ Server 3 ???   │               │ Server 3 ✓     │         │
│   │ - nginx ???    │               │ - nginx 1.24   │         │
│   │ - Python ???   │               │ - Python 3.11  │         │
│   │ - who knows    │               │ - /etc matches │         │
│   └─────────────────┘               └─────────────────┘         │
│                                                                  │
│   KEY BENEFITS:                                                 │
│   ✓ Consistency    - All servers identical                     │
│   ✓ Reproducibility- Rebuild identical systems anytime         │
│   ✓ Audit Trail    - All changes tracked in version control    │
│   ✓ Compliance     - Enforce security policies automatically   │
│   ✓ Speed          - Deploy to 1000 servers as fast as 1       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
22.2 Push vs Pull Models Understanding deployment models
🔄 Push vs Pull Configuration 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PUSH MODEL                 PULL MODEL               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   PUSH:                             PULL:                       │
│   Control node initiates            Nodes fetch their config    │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌───────────┐                     ┌───────────────┐           │
│   │  Control  │                     │ Config Server │           │
│   │   Node    │                     │   (Puppet/    │           │
│   └─────┬─────┘                     │    Chef)      │           │
│         │                           └───────┬───────┘           │
│         │ pushes configs                    │                   │
│         ▼                                   │                   │
│   ┌─────────┐                               │                   │
│   │         │                     ┌─────────┼─────────┐         │
│   ▼         ▼                     ▼         ▼         ▼         │
│ ┌───┐    ┌───┐    ┌───┐       ┌───┐     ┌───┐     ┌───┐       │
│ │ A │    │ B │    │ C │       │ A │◄────│ B │◄────│ C │       │
│ └───┘    └───┘    └───┘       │ ↑ │     │ ↑ │     │ ↑ │       │
│                               │agent    │agent    │agent       │
│                               └───┘     └───┘     └───┘       │
│                                   polls periodically            │
│                                                                  │
│   EXAMPLES:                         EXAMPLES:                   │
│   • Ansible                         • Puppet (agent)            │
│   • Salt (SSH mode)                 • Chef                      │
│   • Fabric                          • Salt (minion)             │
│                                     • CFEngine                   │
│                                                                  │
│   PROS:                             PROS:                       │
│   ✓ Agentless (less overhead)      ✓ Self-healing              │
│   ✓ Simpler architecture           ✓ Scales to 1000s           │
│   ✓ On-demand execution            ✓ Nodes eventually converge │
│                                                                  │
│   CONS:                             CONS:                       │
│   ✗ Need network access            ✗ Agent required             │
│   ✗ Manual trigger needed          ✗ More infrastructure       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
22.6 Tool Selection Guide Choosing the right CM tool
🔧 Configuration Management Tools Comparison 
┌──────────────┬──────────┬────────┬───────────┬──────────────────┐
│ Tool         │ Model    │ Agent  │ Language  │ Best For         │
├──────────────┼──────────┼────────┼───────────┼──────────────────┤
│ Ansible      │ Push     │ No     │ YAML      │ Simple, DevOps   │
│ Puppet       │ Pull     │ Yes    │ DSL       │ Enterprise, Win  │
│ Chef         │ Pull     │ Yes    │ Ruby      │ Complex infra    │
│ Salt         │ Both     │ Opt    │ YAML      │ Speed, scale     │
│ CFEngine     │ Pull     │ Yes    │ DSL       │ Embedded, IoT    │
└──────────────┴──────────┴────────┴───────────┴──────────────────┘

DECISION FLOWCHART:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Need Windows support as priority? ──► Puppet or Ansible         │
│                                                                  │
│ Prefer agentless? ──────────────────► Ansible                   │
│                                                                  │
│ Ruby shop / Complex policies? ──────► Chef                      │
│                                                                  │
│ Need extreme scale (10k+ nodes)? ───► Salt or Puppet            │
│                                                                  │
│ Simple YAML, quick start? ──────────► Ansible                   │
│                                                                  │
│ Enterprise compliance focus? ───────► Puppet                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Automating OS and server configuration

Chapter Topics

23.1 OS Configuration Scripts
23.2 Package Management Automation
